title: Guided Meditation: Difficult for the Self to Understand what it can't do
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with simple orientation to body, breath, and a warm attitude of love, then settles into a steady practice of resting attention without overworking it. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes the difference between what we can intentionally do and what belongs to awareness itself, repeatedly returning to the question of “what is your job” and what is not. The talk moves toward a quiet humility: attention can be directed, but awareness and the unfolding of Dharma cannot be forced or owned by the self.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting attention, distinguishing effort from awareness, and softening the self's claim to control practice.

chapters.title: Arrive in the body
chapters.description: The meditation opens by inviting simple contact with body, breath, and love.

chapters.title: A relaxed attention
chapters.description: The instruction shifts toward a gentle, workable way of pointing attention without strain.

chapters.title: Hope, fear, and re-dedication
chapters.description: Brensilver names the pull of hope and fear and returns again and again to what's actually here.

chapters.title: What is your job
chapters.description: He asks listeners to sort out what they can do from what was never theirs to control.

chapters.title: Staying with the step
chapters.description: The guided practice continues with repeated cues to stay present while not letting attention's doingness obscure awareness.

chapters.title: The self and the path
chapters.description: He reflects on how intentional practice can create the illusion that the self is orchestrating the Dharma path.

chapters.title: Awareness larger than story
chapters.description: The talk opens into the sense that the self's plot is too small to describe what is actually happening.

chapters.title: Instruction and limitation
chapters.description: He closes by acknowledging that instruction implies doing, while reminding listeners not to let action eclipse what cannot be done.

title: Guided Meditation: Awareness is reverential about all phenomena
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation on meeting experience without sorting it into pleasant and unpleasant. The practice moves from settling the body, to softening judgment, to recognizing awareness as naturally respectful of whatever arises. He closes by inviting listeners to trust their own wisdom and to speak to themselves as they would guide another meditator.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, relaxing preferences, and meeting all experience with reverent awareness.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting the listener to adjust, settle, and find a center.

chapters.title: Feeling Good, Feeling Bad
chapters.description: He frames practice as both noticing what feels good and being willing to meet what feels bad consciously.

chapters.title: Releasing Preference
chapters.description: The talk turns to fantasies about pleasure and the tendency to divide experience into pleasant and unpleasant.

chapters.title: Just Breathe
chapters.description: Listeners are asked to relax the strain of sorting experience and simply breathe with what is present.

chapters.title: Reverence for Experience
chapters.description: Awareness is described as respectful of all phenomena, including pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral tones.

chapters.title: Steady Attention
chapters.description: The guidance points toward stable attention and a lighter, less forceful relationship to effort.

chapters.title: Effort and Ease
chapters.description: He explores how effort can be both necessary and beautiful while still allowing a felt sense of ease.

chapters.title: Meeting the Mind
chapters.description: The practice opens into meeting the mind as it is, with improvisational wisdom and less control.

chapters.title: Listening Within
chapters.description: The closing invitation is to ask what guidance one would offer another meditator, then say it to oneself and listen.

title: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a lightly guided meditation on opening to this moment by softening obvious tension and the subtler habits of control. The talk moves from surrender and the release of dividing lines, toward a steadier sense of urgency that is not driven by desperation. It closes with a quiet inquiry into awareness, vulnerability, and what can actually be harmed.
short_summary: A gently guided meditation on surrender, non-delusion, and meeting experience without bracing.

chapters.title: Opening to this moment
chapters.description: He begins by asking what it means to open to the present without immediately tightening around it.

chapters.title: Putting down control
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward releasing grasping, aversion, and the subtler effort to make life happen.

chapters.title: Breath and bracing
chapters.description: He invites the breath to signal safety to the deeper mind and notices the subconscious lines that separate self from experience.

chapters.title: Urgency without strain
chapters.description: The meditation develops a careful sense of impermanence and urgency that does not collapse into self-pressure.

chapters.title: Awake, not deliberate
chapters.description: He distinguishes vivid awareness from technique and points to non-delusion as something deeper than method.

chapters.title: Awareness and vulnerability
chapters.description: The talk settles into the possibility that awareness does not shield us from life, but can change how it is met.

chapters.title: What can be harmed
chapters.description: It ends with a brief, open inquiry into harm, fragility, and the meeting of one gust of wind with another.

title: Guided Meditation: Dedicating our practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation on arriving, softening, and dedicating practice to the welfare of self and others. The instruction moves from settling body and mind, to letting go of bracing and defensiveness, to reflecting on love, finitude, and the seriousness of practice without strain. The tone stays steady and unforced, with long stretches of repeated phrases that support breathing and attention.
short_summary: A guided meditation on arriving, softening, and dedicating practice with love and clarity about finitude.

chapters.title: Arriving Here
chapters.description: The meditation opens by asking what it means for body, mind, and heart to arrive in this moment.

chapters.title: Putting It Down
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward releasing bracing and defensiveness so experience can be met more directly.

chapters.title: Dedicating Practice
chapters.description: Brensilver invites reflection on whom we practice for and on the seriousness of practice in light of birth and death.

chapters.title: Love and Finitude
chapters.description: The talk explores how love may be larger than our power while still remaining steady and strong.

chapters.title: Quiet Repetition
chapters.description: A long guided stretch uses repeated phrases to let love gather in the background while breathing and honoring the path.

chapters.title: Seeing Differently
chapters.description: The meditation closes by linking love, finitude, and the way the world looks when they come into clearer view.

title: Guided Meditation: safe to relax
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a gentle calibration of effort: not too much, not too little, but what this moment asks for. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to breathing in a way that signals safety to the body-mind, so that even amid vulnerability and imperfection it can be okay to relax and let go. Midway through, he brings in a brief Rumi reference to clarify the difference between ordinary rest and the release that comes from stepping out of self-view and possession.
short_summary: A guided meditation on calibrating effort so the mind can trust relaxation and let go of identification.

chapters.title: Calibrating Effort
chapters.description: He begins by asking listeners to sense how much effort the moment calls for, without turning that into a fixed right or wrong answer.

chapters.title: Ease in Practice
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into offering effort from a place of ease rather than from self-view, with repeated reminders to breathe.

chapters.title: Safety in the Breath
chapters.description: Breath becomes a way of cueing the deep mind that it is okay to soften even while imperfection, fragility, and uncertainty remain present.

chapters.title: Rumi and Rest
chapters.description: A brief quote from Rumi frames true rest as release from entanglement, which Matthew then connects to Buddhist language about stepping out of identification.

chapters.title: Making No Objects
chapters.description: He points to how aversion and delusion turn life into objects, and to the measurelessness that can appear in letting go.

chapters.title: Trusting Release
chapters.description: The closing instruction returns to not settling on the vantage point created by identification, ending with trust in letting go.

title: Guided Meditation: Refuge
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation on refuge, beginning with repeated invitations to settle in Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, and to let love, gratitude, or reverence soften the heart. The practice gradually widens from the immediate body and breath to a larger sense of being held by the stream of Dharma across time, before ending with a brief reflective closing that points toward the split between subject and object. The tone stays simple and intimate, with long stretches of repetition and spaciousness rather than a lot of discursive explanation.
short_summary: A guided meditation on refuge that moves from calming the heart to resting in a larger view of Dharma and life.

chapters.title: Entering Refuge
chapters.description: The meditation begins with repeated invitations to take refuge and set aside concern about anything outside that frame.

chapters.title: Love and Reverence
chapters.description: Matthew asks listeners to call in love, gratitude, or reverence as a way to signal the deep mind to relax and let go.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: Attention is guided to a resting place in the body, breath, and the felt edge between body and space, with an emphasis on simplicity.

chapters.title: Return to Refuge
chapters.description: The refrain of refuge continues, holding the practice in a steady, repetitive rhythm.

chapters.title: Dharma Across Time
chapters.description: The talk opens into a broader perspective, suggesting that Dharma has held countless moments and that a life needs a vantage point larger than the life itself.

chapters.title: Less Identified
chapters.description: Matthew describes how loosening identification with one's circle of concern can make the mind more spacious and less pinched by time.

chapters.title: Closing Insight
chapters.description: The closing turns briefly to a Krishnamurti quote about the split between subject and object, then ends in bell sounds and silence.

title: Guided Meditation: non-referentiality
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation that gradually shifts from settling with a subtly pleasant sensation into a broader invitation to release reference points altogether. The practice moves through body-based cues, softening the mind’s tendency to measure and orient, and then toward a sense of unpartitioned space, non-referential awareness, and less bracing against pain or time. Near the end, the language becomes more spacious and poetic, with reminders that this moment is innocent and that the trees, as a final image, seem to arrive where they are going.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, softening orientation, and opening into non-referential awareness.

chapters.title: Settling with the pleasant
chapters.description: The practice begins by finding something subtly pleasant and letting that be enough, even if it exists alongside difficulty.

chapters.title: A soft smile
chapters.description: He suggests a tiny, almost invisible smile and noticing how bodily ripples can shape the mind's tone.

chapters.title: Dropping the vantage point
chapters.description: The meditation questions the habit of taking a position inside experience and begins to loosen the sense of body-mind as fixed territory.

chapters.title: Renounce orientation
chapters.description: The instruction turns more direct, inviting a release of representation and referentiality and a return to immediate sensation.

chapters.title: Fear and love
chapters.description: He contrasts the narrowing of fear with the leveling quality of love, then points to the ways we keep score even in this moment.

chapters.title: Relaxing more deeply
chapters.description: The meditation continues in a quieter register, inviting deeper relaxation and a less defended relationship to experience.

chapters.title: Not bracing against pain
chapters.description: The emphasis returns to bodily sensation and the practice of not bracing against time or pain.

chapters.title: The grammar of self
chapters.description: He points to 'away from me' as an artifact of grammar, loosening the reflexive structure of ownership and separation.

chapters.title: Trees and arrival
chapters.description: The closing image evokes trees and future journeys as a gentle way of sensing movement without strain or self-reference.

title: Guided Meditation: The Safety of Letting Go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that moves from settling the body into a deeper kind of listening, where thought is seen less as a problem than as a protective habit. He gradually reframes thinking, emotion, and even control itself as things that can be met with permission, forgiveness, and mindfulness. The talk ends by opening into the safety that can come from relinquishing control and resting with experience as it is.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling the body, softening the mind’s grip, and discovering the safety of letting go.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: Begin by finding posture and allowing the breath to enliven numbness and soothe hyper-activation.

chapters.title: Unifying the Body
chapters.description: The practice turns toward gentle unification, letting the whole body be touched by the Dharma without demanding that everything feel pleasant.

chapters.title: Deep Listening
chapters.description: Attention shifts to hearing thought itself, including moments of listening outward toward silence and inward toward mental talk.

chapters.title: Thinking as Protection
chapters.description: Thought is presented as a safety behavior—an innocent way the mind checks threat and opportunity—and the practice becomes forgiving the mind and returning to listening.

chapters.title: Permission for Feeling
chapters.description: The meditation opens to bodily affect and emotional alarm, allowing sensations to arise without needing to manage or change them.

chapters.title: This Moment
chapters.description: A prolonged refrain of 'this moment' settles the listener into presence, emphasizing immediacy, imperfection, and poignancy.

chapters.title: Relinquishing Control
chapters.description: The talk widens into the recognition that life eventually outruns our control, and practice becomes willingly opening to reality and the safety of letting go.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting in Goodness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens with a brief, wry set of Melissa Broder quotations that hold dread and humor together, then turns toward a guided meditation on settling and appreciating what is already present. The practice moves from posture and breath to resting with goodness in the body, noticing ordinary life as quietly sufficient, and softening the mind’s habit of organizing around distress. In the closing stretch, he ties enjoyment, letting go, and the Dharma idea of anicca together, suggesting that fear and worry can relax when the mind stops needing to manage everything.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from humor and existential unease into settling, breath, appreciation, and the quiet Dharma of enjoyment.

chapters.title: Comic Prelude
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Melissa Broder’s bleak, funny lines to frame the talk with a mix of dread, self-awareness, and humor.

chapters.title: Finding Posture
chapters.description: He invites the group into stillness and gently shifts from the opening mood into the meditation seat.

chapters.title: Settling in Goodness
chapters.description: The practice turns toward breathing, relaxing, and noticing the goodness around and within the body without needing to fix it.

chapters.title: Resting With Breath
chapters.description: He continues the guided settling, encouraging the body to remember how to relax and take in the breath.

chapters.title: Being Here
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the relief of waking up to ordinary life and the subtle elation of simply being present.

chapters.title: Enjoyment Deeply
chapters.description: He repeats the teaching that enjoyment may be the deepest way of life, giving delight the force of a Dharma orientation.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: The meditation softens toward release, with enjoyment, tranquility, and love arising after grasping eases.

chapters.title: Fear and Uncertainty
chapters.description: Matthew names fear and worry as meaning-making around anicca, then points toward the relief that follows letting go.

title: Guided Meditation: Sitting as if already dead
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation uses the image of sitting "as if already dead" to loosen concern, self-protection, and future bargaining, while keeping the mind very awake. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to softening the body, relaxing the heart, and resting in a way that lets love remain when fear and hatred lose their footing. The practice gradually widens into loving-kindness and a simple instruction to slow down, be still, and let the moment do some of the work.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing self-protection, resting in awake stillness, and letting loving-kindness become the organizing cue.

chapters.title: Settling and relaxing
chapters.description: The meditation begins by relaxing whatever can be relaxed and finding a breath that soothes the skin.

chapters.title: Softening the heart
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward subtly relaxing the heart and attuning to the body's quieter soothing effects.

chapters.title: Space from samsara
chapters.description: Brensilver invites a little distance from old habits and the ways we come into practice, emphasizing being softened by the moment.

chapters.title: Not bartering with future
chapters.description: He encourages not negotiating with the future for now, making room for present-moment attention instead of worry.

chapters.title: Already dead, awake
chapters.description: The core framing arrives: sit as if already dead, not extrapolating the instruction into life planning, but staying vividly awake.

chapters.title: Love without fear
chapters.description: In the imagined afterlife of no fear, self-identity and hatred loosen, leaving rest and love as the available conditions.

chapters.title: Mettā made vast
chapters.description: Loving-kindness is described as making the mind vast and measureless, with no other side to this love.

chapters.title: Hatred is not final
chapters.description: The closing reflection says hatred is never the last word, and ends with a quiet instruction to slow down and be still.

title: Guided Practice: Compassion and Equanimity (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided practice and reflection on compassion and equanimity as two qualities that need to be held in conversation, not treated as a fixed formula. He names compassion as love in the face of suffering and equanimity as love in the face of helplessness, then moves into a brief guided settling, a prayer-like aspiration, and a longer contemplation on grief, mortality, and the limits of what love can change. The talk closes by returning to compassion, with an emphasis on resting without collapse and meeting suffering skillfully.
short_summary: A guided Dharma talk on balancing compassion with equanimity, from grief and helplessness back into renewed care.

chapters.title: Middle path, living balance
chapters.description: He opens by framing the Dharma as a dynamic balance between wholesome forces rather than a single rule to follow.

chapters.title: Compassion and equanimity
chapters.description: Matthew defines the two qualities and explores how love must respond both to suffering and to helplessness.

chapters.title: Settling into practice
chapters.description: He briefly adjusts the room, then guides a short period of relaxation and a prayerful aspiration for lessening suffering.

chapters.title: The trembling heart
chapters.description: The talk turns to the heart’s trembling and the way stillness can support the development of love.

chapters.title: Love and mortality
chapters.description: He reflects on death, the limits of control, and the possibility that love remains one answer when other consolations fall away.

chapters.title: Return to compassion
chapters.description: After resting in equanimity, he doubles back to compassion and closes with a wish to meet suffering skillfully and bring more safety and joy.

title: Guided Meditation: What Does it Take to Land Here? (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with a brief retreat ritual of surrendering phones and settling into shared silence. Matthew Brensilver then leads a practice centered on letting go safely: meeting fear with awareness, trusting the support of the ground, and allowing the breath to signal ease to the body and mind. In the second half, he shifts to walking meditation, emphasizing simplicity, rhythm, and the felt sense of experience over discursive thinking. The talk closes with practical guidance for where to walk at Spirit Rock and how to adapt the practice if walking is difficult.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting go, embodied awareness, and simple walking practice.

chapters.title: Opening Ceremony
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes the retreat and explains the first-morning phone surrender as a symbolic release into practice.

chapters.title: Settling Into Silence
chapters.description: After the phone ceremony and bells, the group settles into shared stillness before instructions begin.

chapters.title: Letting Go Safely
chapters.description: He asks what needs blessing with awareness so that letting go can feel less threatening and more possible.

chapters.title: Honest Practice
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes not pretending, trusting the Dharma to hold the fullness of life, and breathing with awareness.

chapters.title: Breath and Fear
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward feeling how thought can arise from fear and using the breath to soothe agitation.

chapters.title: Deepening Into Rest
chapters.description: Longer stretches of quiet are framed as practice in simplicity, acceptance, and a calmer relationship to the mind.

chapters.title: Walking Meditation
chapters.description: Matthew introduces walking meditation as a simple rhythm of standing, stepping, turning, and sensing the body directly.

chapters.title: Where to Walk
chapters.description: He gives practical directions for the courtyard and meadow, and offers adaptations for people with mobility limits.

title: Guided Metta meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet introduction to metta as a flexible heart practice rather than a feeling to force. He emphasizes that the Dharma does not ask us to pretend, and that loving-kindness may take different forms depending on the moment: compassion, joy, equanimity, forgiveness, gratitude, or simply a simpler phrase that can be held with sincerity. The talk moves from this framing into guided practice, then into questions about bodily constriction, deservingness, grief, and how love and sorrow are intertwined.
short_summary: A guided metta talk that treats loving-kindness as an adaptable practice of truth, not emotional performance.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the session and sets up metta as an improvisational practice that should not require pretending.

chapters.title: Finding the right love
chapters.description: He names several forms of heart practice and warns against measuring practice only by the intensity of feeling.

chapters.title: Measureless awareness
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Brahma-viharas, concentration, and purification, including a reference to Ajahn Sucitto on moving out of the gridlock of self.

chapters.title: Guided metta practice
chapters.description: Matthew leads a long sitting practice, inviting attention into the body, the heart center, and whatever words or images can orient the mind toward goodness.

chapters.title: Practice in the world
chapters.description: In a later section of questions, he returns to the four Brahma-viharas as different kinds of love that may be relevant while moving through the world.

chapters.title: Constriction and worthiness
chapters.description: He answers a question about bodily constriction and the feeling of being undeserving, pointing toward a practice that steps outside the deserving versus undeserving debate.

chapters.title: Love and grief
chapters.description: The final discussion takes up the tension between personal ease and world suffering, and Matthew argues that grief is an expression of love rather than a defect in it.

chapters.title: Closing instructions
chapters.description: He settles the group back into quiet practice and closes with practical guidance for walking, stretching, and letting different forms of love become available.

title: Guided Meditation: entering the dharma realm
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that frames practice as intentionally “entering a Dharma realm,” a field of mind shaped by goodness, kindness, clarity, and letting go. He moves through refuge, the subtle reorientation of perception, and the way this ground helps us meet suffering without adding more to it. The talk closes by turning toward love, vow, and service as expressions of a radical non-violent path.
short_summary: A guided meditation on shifting into a Dharma field where refuge, love, and clarity change how suffering is met.

chapters.title: Taking Refuge
chapters.description: The meditation begins by settling into refuge as a way of meeting the world without becoming overwhelmed by it.

chapters.title: Why Refuge Matters
chapters.description: Brensilver reflects on how hatred and confusion shape us, and why we need repeated remembrance of refuge and non-harming.

chapters.title: Entering the Dharma Field
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of consciously entering a Dharma realm, where body, thought, and feeling are organized by practice.

chapters.title: Second Arrow
chapters.description: The talk turns to the second arrow teaching, distinguishing unavoidable suffering from the suffering added around it.

chapters.title: Goodwill and Renunciation
chapters.description: He emphasizes abiding together in goodwill, renunciation, and the intention not to harm.

chapters.title: Subtle Change
chapters.description: Using Shinzen Young’s phrase, he points out that these shifts can be very small yet still significant.

chapters.title: Breath and Love
chapters.description: Attention settles into cues of goodness, including images of what is loved and a conscious breath that recalls love.

chapters.title: What Love Asks
chapters.description: The closing questions ask what love asks of us, what we vow, and what we are here to serve.

title: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a lightly guided meditation on relaxing the body and loosening control so experience can be met more simply. The talk moves from basic instructions to a reflection on equanimity, non-proliferation, and how unpleasant experience can be allowed to end without turning into a story. It closes with a gentler, more embodied image of the moment as something that can soften the body-mind and strengthen the case for love.
short_summary: A softly guided meditation on relaxing control, meeting experience plainly, and resting in equanimity.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting relaxation in the body and a loosening of control.

chapters.title: Putting down identity
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward forgetting, even briefly, who we think we are and releasing habitual reactions.

chapters.title: Letting experience be
chapters.description: The talk deepens into allowing pleasant and unpleasant sensations to be exactly what they are, without adding extra interpretation.

chapters.title: Equanimity and non-proliferation
chapters.description: Brensilver describes equanimity as what lets experience stop at itself instead of becoming a problem or command.

chapters.title: Very alive to this moment
chapters.description: The repeated phrase emphasizes a vivid, present awareness that does not need to elaborate.

chapters.title: Softening into love
chapters.description: A closing image of the moment as a skilled healer frames discomfort as something that can support tenderness and courage.

chapters.title: Closing bell meditation
chapters.description: The remainder is quiet closing time marked by bells and space for the practice to settle.

title: Guided Meditation: Sound and Silence
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on listening as a way of meeting sound, thought, and silence without grasping. The practice moves from simple instruction into a wider field of hearing, including external sounds, internal sounds, equanimity, and a gentle bowing to fear and preoccupation. Near the end, the attention turns toward very distant sounds and the edge where hearing and silence are no longer easy to separate.
short_summary: A guided meditation on listening deeply to sound, thought, and silence with increasing equanimity.

chapters.title: Entering Listening
chapters.description: He introduces the practice as a simple act of listening and invites settling into sound without extra effort.

chapters.title: Hunger and Sound
chapters.description: The talk explores how hearing can feel less hungry than seeing or thinking, opening a different relationship to experience.

chapters.title: Listening in All Directions
chapters.description: Attention widens to sounds around, behind, above, and below, with silence becoming more noticeable.

chapters.title: Self With No Job
chapters.description: He points to how the sense of self has little to do and encourages a kind of listening that does not depend on identity or role.

chapters.title: No Inside or Outside
chapters.description: External sound and internal sound are treated alike, with an emphasis on not propping up awareness or getting caught in grammar.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Nonclinging
chapters.description: The practice becomes one of not picking up phenomena, letting sounds and thoughts remain present without becoming sticky.

chapters.title: Bowing to Preoccupations
chapters.description: He suggests bowing to fear, needs, and preoccupations so they can be held with less neglect and less chewing.

chapters.title: Edge of Silence
chapters.description: The meditation closes by experimenting with distant sounds and the moment when it is hard to tell whether one is hearing sound or silence.

title: Guided Meditation: Ease Makes Letting Go Safer
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a gentler relationship with agitation by finding a breath that carries ease into the body. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes that awareness does not solve every problem, but it can change how problems are carried, making letting go feel safer when there is some stability and warmth present. The practice moves gradually from settling the body to resting in a subtle sense of care, simplicity, and enoughness.
short_summary: A guided meditation on using ease, stability, and warmth to make letting go feel safer.

chapters.title: Settling the breath
chapters.description: The practice begins by finding a way of breathing that signals ease to the body.

chapters.title: Rest with ease
chapters.description: Rather than trying to remove agitation, the instruction is to rest attention in any place that feels soothed.

chapters.title: Care for the present
chapters.description: The talk links confidence about the future with taking very good care of what is here now.

chapters.title: Awareness changes carrying
chapters.description: Agitation is named as something that wants solving, but awareness is presented as dissolving the weight of experience rather than fixing every problem.

chapters.title: Wide world, simple attention
chapters.description: The meditation widens the frame to the vastness of the world while keeping attention simple, relaxed, and buoyed by care.

chapters.title: Letting go feels safer
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward the felt sense that letting go can become safer when ease, warmth, and stability are present.

chapters.title: Warmth and abiding
chapters.description: The closing settles into subtle warmth, a tiny smile, and the sense that simply abiding is enough.

title: Guided Meditation: Activity and Receptivity
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on finding a workable balance between activity and receptivity. Using the image of entering water, he explores how the body gives consent to letting go, how faith and safety shape surrender, and how attention can be directed without becoming rigid. The practice widens into noticing when effort is skillful and when receptivity becomes the steadier ground.
short_summary: A guided meditation on balancing directed attention with receptivity, grounded in bodily consent, trust, and letting go.

chapters.title: Entering Gradually
chapters.description: The meditation begins with the image of easing into water as a way of approaching practice gently rather than forcing immediacy.

chapters.title: Body Consent
chapters.description: He turns to the body's need for permission and safety, framing letting go as something that must feel okay to the animal body.

chapters.title: Illusions of Control
chapters.description: The talk explores the contradictions of letting go and the recognition that grasping often rests on an illusion of control.

chapters.title: Doing and Surrender
chapters.description: He describes the tension between giving and receiving, effort and trust, and the sense that awareness does not depend on personal control.

chapters.title: Balancing the Mind
chapters.description: The focus shifts to how different moments call for different balances between activity and receptivity in meditation.

chapters.title: Against Hierarchy
chapters.description: He cautions against mental hierarchies that entangle experience with grasping and points toward practice shaped by present causes and conditions.

chapters.title: Non-Distraction
chapters.description: The teaching names a quieter stability in which phenomena are less sticky and the lure of meaning-making begins to fade.

chapters.title: Compassionate Balance
chapters.description: The meditation closes with compassion for the human pull toward hopes, fears, and meaning-making, and with a reminder to love our way through the balance of activity and receptivity.

title: Guided Meditation: Bright, Steady, Open
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on bright, steady, open awareness: knowing experience without collapsing into the self. He emphasizes practicing with clarity, stability, equanimity, and a gentle relationship to conditioning, then widens the frame toward rest, humility, and spaciousness. The meditation closes by returning to the simple fact of being neither everything nor nothing, but present and responsive.
short_summary: A guided meditation on bright, steady, open awareness, gentle effort, and resting in spaciousness.

chapters.title: Settling Into Context
chapters.description: After brief introductions, the talk opens by shifting attention from what seems in front of us to what is all around and through us.

chapters.title: Self and Suffering
chapters.description: Matthew frames suffering as a collapse into self-centered gravity and points toward freedom as a larger, less contracted way of knowing.

chapters.title: Brightness and Stability
chapters.description: The practice turns toward clear, steady awareness, with an emphasis on knowing phenomena without making them about oneself.

chapters.title: Permission to Practice
chapters.description: He invites a relaxed attitude of deep permission and suggests that the practice may be possible because it is shared and supported together.

chapters.title: Time as Ally
chapters.description: When the mind is already clear and stable, there is nothing to force; he introduces the idea of letting time support the unfolding, including a brief mention of Shinzen Young.

chapters.title: Humility and Effort
chapters.description: The guidance deepens into humility before conditioning, bowing to it without yielding to it, and finding the narrow place where gentle effort can live.

chapters.title: Opening the Frame
chapters.description: The meditation widens into a less self-referential view, inviting contact with a larger perspective that holds our smallness without erasing us.

chapters.title: Not Much, Not Nothing
chapters.description: The closing reflection rests in the paradox of being neither grand nor negligible, but a presence that can increasingly meet life everywhere.

title: Guided Meditation: Relax and release
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation on relaxing body and mind, with attention to the ways future-thinking, control, and spiritual striving can tighten experience. The practice moves toward letting go more deliberately, including a gentle but sometimes firmer sense of renunciation, and then into refuge and a simple resting with meaning-making set aside. The talk stays close to the felt experience of this moment, returning again and again to stability, clarity, and enoughness.
short_summary: A guided practice on relaxing effort, releasing control, and settling into refuge and enoughness.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: An opening invitation to relax body and mind and begin the practice.

chapters.title: Present Moment
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the burden of future-thinking and the invitation to fall into what is here now.

chapters.title: Steady Attention
chapters.description: Attention is directed skillfully to support stability, clarity, and alertness without becoming rigid.

chapters.title: Not Clinging
chapters.description: The guidance warns against turning technique itself into another form of grasping, control, or aversion.

chapters.title: Renouncing Effort
chapters.description: Brensilver contrasts endless problem-solving with the practice of letting go, which may be gentle or firm.

chapters.title: What We Carry
chapters.description: The meditation reflects on the habits of ego and the ways we try to keep things together.

chapters.title: Taking Refuge
chapters.description: The instruction shifts toward Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha as a refuge that is not dependent on circumstances.

chapters.title: No Need to Mean
chapters.description: The practice opens into a quieter stance of not insisting on meaning and letting things be just what they are.

chapters.title: Closing Rest
chapters.description: The meditation closes in silence and a soft settling, with the final guidance thinning to almost nothing.

title: Guided Meditation: Allowing (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a very simple release: forgive all phenomena, including the movements of liking, disliking, and selfing. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to not fighting inner conflict, then gradually opens the practice into softening, trusting awareness, and letting the present moment be exactly as it is. In the closing period he offers a strong permission practice—nothing done in these minutes is wrong—before ending with brief retreat announcements.
short_summary: A guided meditation on forgiving experience, softening resistance, and allowing whatever arises without self-judgment.

chapters.title: Opening forgiveness
chapters.description: The meditation begins with the instruction to forgive all phenomena, including love, hate, sound, and self.

chapters.title: Not fighting resistance
chapters.description: Matthew reframes practice as a refusal to add another layer of struggle, including the instruction not to fight the fact of fighting.

chapters.title: Softening into Dharma
chapters.description: He names how deeply the heart can resist suffering and invites relaxing, softening, and letting the goodness of Dharma permeate the body.

chapters.title: Settling the spasms
chapters.description: The talk points to spasms of self and fear, and to the habit of fidgeting for relief instead of letting the heart rest.

chapters.title: No autobiography needed
chapters.description: Listeners are told not to turn the moment into another chapter of personal story or meaning-making.

chapters.title: Yielding to awareness
chapters.description: Thoughts, sounds, and sensations are described as territory that can be given over to awareness rather than held as self.

chapters.title: Nothing is wrong
chapters.description: In the final minutes he offers a radical permission practice: loving, hating, grasping, focusing, or drifting are all treated as allowable.

chapters.title: Closing announcements
chapters.description: After the meditation, retreat housekeeping and a cautionary story about a harmful note lead into a reminder to stay careful and quiet.

title: Guided Meditation: Relax a Little Further (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided retreat meditation built around one repeated instruction: relax a little further. He links that softening to letting go of assumptions about self, progress, and control, and to recognizing practice as a balance between effort and receptivity. As the meditation deepens, he turns toward attention, pain, and refuge, ending with a quiet reminder that simply being alive is enough.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing further, softening control, and meeting experience with receptivity and care.

chapters.title: Relax further
chapters.description: The meditation begins with repeated invitations to loosen assumptions about identity, progress, and control.

chapters.title: Doing and surrender
chapters.description: He frames practice as a gentle turn of attention that unfolds within surrender and the dissolving of willful doing.

chapters.title: Attitude and renunciation
chapters.description: The talk continues into wise attention, renunciation, and the effort to keep practice from being hijacked by control.

chapters.title: Attention opens
chapters.description: Matthew shifts to the aperture of attention and how steadiness of heart-mind can make the anchor feel spacious.

chapters.title: Care for pain
chapters.description: He names the self-drama of arrogance and self-hate and asks for exquisite care toward pain instead.

chapters.title: Receiving what is here
chapters.description: The meditation returns to the central rhythm of practice as a movement between doing and receiving.

chapters.title: Love without preference
chapters.description: He points toward loving suffering and bliss equally, with the heart opening to what has not yet been loved.

chapters.title: Refuge everywhere
chapters.description: The talk culminates in the sense that refuge is not bound to a special state or place, but can be found everywhere.

chapters.title: Retreat notes and close
chapters.description: Matthew closes with logistics for the retreat and a final reflection on night sitting, aliveness, and well-wishing.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing all clinging
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation explores relaxation as a form of surrender rather than mere physical softening. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to letting go of control, stepping out of narrow identifications, and allowing the mind to rest without feeding familiar grooves of worry and selfing. The practice moves toward tenderness, forgiveness, and trust in the goodness already present, ending in a quieter sense of awareness less dominated by self.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing clinging by softening control, loosening identification, and trusting the goodness already present.

chapters.title: Settling into surrender
chapters.description: The meditation opens by defining relaxation as surrender and letting go of the agenda to control.

chapters.title: Releasing the familiar
chapters.description: The practice turns toward how much of experience can relax, release, and step out of habitual self-protection.

chapters.title: Fatigue with old patterns
chapters.description: He points to becoming tired of the same worries and stories, and to attuning to silence instead of feeding the mind.

chapters.title: Longing to rest
chapters.description: The guidance frames relaxation as a deep, natural desire to get cozy and release after a long day or a long life.

chapters.title: Stepping out of identification
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes that real ease comes from disidentifying from thought, feeling, and perception and leaving compression behind.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and kindness
chapters.description: The practice softens into deep forgiveness and kindness for conditioning and for the mind’s habitual ways of finding bearings.

chapters.title: Trusting goodness
chapters.description: He encourages relaxing because seeds of goodness are already here, and the sense of self becomes less solid in awareness.

chapters.title: Quiet ending
chapters.description: The meditation closes in a quieter, more spacious awareness, followed by the bell.

title: Guided Meditation: How distant is freedom?
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins by turning attention toward a felt sense of freedom and asking how distance itself is created by the mind. Matthew Brensilver invites a gentle, less controlling relationship to breath and body, then widens into lineage, trust, and the recognition that mindfulness is not another project of self-management. The talk settles into a quiet instruction to rest in the present moment without keeping constant tabs on what is right or wrong.
short_summary: A guided meditation on how ideas of control and distance keep freedom feeling far away, and how to meet the present with more trust.

chapters.title: How Far Is Peace
chapters.description: The meditation opens with the central question of how far away peace or freedom really is.

chapters.title: All Practice Is Here
chapters.description: He invites a felt sense of everything already given to practice—effort, renunciation, suffering, love, and willingness.

chapters.title: Taking Our Cues
chapters.description: Attention turns to lineage and the shelter of a long tradition, then to breathing and feeling the body.

chapters.title: Softening Control
chapters.description: He explores how to orient attention toward the breath with the right mix of precision and ease, and how relaxing control reduces tension.

chapters.title: Boundaries Without Grasping
chapters.description: The instruction shifts toward giving the mind boundaries without turning meditation into another form of control.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Ungovernability
chapters.description: He names mindfulness as a recognition of what we do not govern and a harmonizing of the heart with that fact.

chapters.title: Trusting the Moment
chapters.description: The teaching moves into a steadier confidence that there is already more than enough, and that we need not go searching for goodness.

chapters.title: Resting in Silence
chapters.description: The final section emphasizes trust that the moment will not harm the heart, letting go of compulsive checking and taking cues from silence.

title: Guided Meditation: Internal and External Sound and Silence
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on listening: to external sound, internal sound, and the silence around and within them. The practice moves gently from noticing the field of hearing to softening identification with thinking, then toward receptivity, non-preference, and a quieter sense of presence. He also draws on a Shakespeare line, a brief Mother Teresa story, and Martin Luther King Jr. to underline how listening can be both contemplative and ethical.
short_summary: A guided meditation on sound, silence, and the habit of releasing preference and narrative.

chapters.title: Entering Silence
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting silence to move through awareness while noticing nearby sounds without strain.

chapters.title: Outer and Inner Sound
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the difference between external sounds and the internal sounds of thinking, including how they are heard.

chapters.title: No Preference
chapters.description: The practice widens into hearing sound and silence without preference, allowing thoughts and quiet to be equally included.

chapters.title: Receptive Listening
chapters.description: He emphasizes simple wakefulness and uses Shakespeare to question how much meaning the mind adds to sound and thought.

chapters.title: Body and Space
chapters.description: Attention softens into the felt boundary of the body, experienced as porous and gradated rather than fixed.

chapters.title: Meaning and Fear
chapters.description: Matthew names the habit of narrating experience and suggests it often grows out of fear and the need to make meaning.

chapters.title: Dharma as Blessing
chapters.description: He briefly opens into gratitude for the Dharma and the surprising conditions that allow practice to unfold.

chapters.title: Listening in Prayer
chapters.description: A short Mother Teresa story frames prayer as listening, landing on the line that this is how we listen regardless of theology.

chapters.title: Nonviolence in Practice
chapters.description: The talk returns to non-violence toward phenomena, invokes Martin Luther King Jr., and closes by saying that this is how we listen.

title: Guided Meditation: Forgiving Body, Mind and World
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle guided meditation built around forgiveness as a practical loosening of friction rather than a forced state of peace. The practice moves from sensing what the mind needs, through body-based softening and truce with pain, toward a spacious awareness that can hold reactions, fear, hope, and ordinary sounds without struggle. It ends with a quiet inquiry into what awareness remains even when control drops away.
short_summary: A guided meditation on forgiving body, mind, and world, gradually softening into spacious awareness.

chapters.title: Settling into need
chapters.description: The meditation begins by distinguishing what the mind needs from what it craves and framing practice as both peace and the willingness to meet difficulty.

chapters.title: Making peace
chapters.description: He reframes practice as making peace with pain, helplessness, grasping, and aversion rather than trying to force perfect calm.

chapters.title: Forgive body and mind
chapters.description: The instruction turns directly toward forgiving the body, the mind, sorrow, the human condition, and the burden of past, pain, and death.

chapters.title: Global softening
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to a whole-body relaxation and a refusal to fight oneself, replacing control with a simpler field of forgiveness.

chapters.title: Nothing left to do
chapters.description: After forgiving everything, the practice points to abiding with whatever remains when craving and hatred no longer need to do their work.

chapters.title: Breath and non-friction
chapters.description: The guided meditation deepens into natural breathing, softening, and a steady, low-friction attention that can rest in the body.

chapters.title: Everything tastes like Dharma
chapters.description: Awareness broadens to include reactions, longing, aversion, fear, hope, and even ordinary sounds as part of one continuous field of practice.

chapters.title: Rest in awareness
chapters.description: The closing inquiry asks what remains when the bell rings and whether there is an awareness that does not depend on effort, ending in simple resting.

title: Guided Meditation: Urgency of Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on the "urgency" that can arise from feeling. The practice begins with settling the body and breathing, then turns toward noticing hidden agendas, control strategies, and the way emotional surges can seem like commands. As the meditation deepens, he repeatedly returns to relaxing, absorbing feeling, and letting breathing become safe again.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting emotional surges without turning them into commands to control experience.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The meditation opens by softening the body through fuller breaths and a soothing exhale.

chapters.title: Relaxation cues
chapters.description: Attention moves through the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and a sense of brightness in the spine as the body yields to earth and sky.

chapters.title: Hidden agendas
chapters.description: The teaching invites noticing the semi-conscious agendas and efforts that keep trying to fix pain, peace, or the future.

chapters.title: Feeling as urgency
chapters.description: Brensilver describes how bodily feeling creates a sense that something must happen, and how to let feeling remain feeling.

chapters.title: False alarms
chapters.description: He frames sensations and phenomena as false alarms and emphasizes the difference between control and letting go.

chapters.title: Breath and equanimity
chapters.description: The practice returns to the breath, with repeated reminders to feel breathing in a relaxed way and to stay with each beginning, middle, and end.

chapters.title: Relaxing the threat
chapters.description: The closing section focuses on tension as an innocent control impulse and on relaxing the thorny quality of feeling until breathing feels safe again.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing and Letting Feeling be Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a simple, careful relationship to pleasant and unpleasant feeling: know it as it is, and notice what softens when it no longer has to mean something about you. The instruction moves from relaxing obvious tension into a narrower and then wider field of attention, including breath, body, and difficult experience with a light touch of kindness. It ends with a quiet emphasis on not bracing against experience and on the felt necessity of love.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting pleasant and unpleasant feeling directly, relaxing tension, and including experience with steadiness and kindness.

chapters.title: Know Feeling Simply
chapters.description: The meditation begins by naming pleasant and unpleasant feeling as they are, without needing to do anything else with them.

chapters.title: Relax the Urgency
chapters.description: Matthew repeats the invitation to let sensations mean less about identity, and notices how this allows the mind to relax.

chapters.title: Soften the Body
chapters.description: Attention turns to relaxing what can be relaxed, especially tension in the mind, jaw, and belly.

chapters.title: Feel the Breath
chapters.description: The guidance shifts toward the felt body and the natural rhythm of breathing, with subtle imagery used to support direct sensation.

chapters.title: Uncontrived Breathing
chapters.description: The breath is approached as unforced and uncontrolled, with tension understood as a cue for further softening.

chapters.title: Subtle In-Breath and Out
chapters.description: The in-breath and out-breath are noticed for their small effects of brightening and soothing, with attention to subtlety.

chapters.title: Widen the Aperture
chapters.description: The practice alternates between a small focus on the breath and a wider field that can hold pain, fear, fatigue, and confusion with mindfulness and love.

chapters.title: Radical Inclusion
chapters.description: The teaching states that Dharma does not ask us to pretend, but to include what is here and find a sense of belonging with experience.

chapters.title: Necessity of Love
chapters.description: Near the end of the sit, the talk points toward the simple dawning that love is necessary, and closes with a final reminder not to brace.

title: Guided Meditation: moving towards compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation on how compassion can grow from directly meeting suffering rather than turning away from it. He begins with an Issa poem about dukkha and then repeatedly returns to simple supports: settling the body, soothing the heart, and allowing mindfulness to include sorrow and happiness. The practice gradually widens into a steadier wish for less suffering, with imagery, silent phrases, and a sense of protecting love as it meets pain.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting suffering directly and letting compassion take shape through stillness, mindfulness, and quiet inner phrases.

chapters.title: Opening and Issa poem
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the theme with a short poem by Kobayashi Issa and uses it to frame the coexistence of suffering and openness.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided practice begins by settling into posture and asking what conditions support love, including tranquility, collectedness, and temporarily putting the world down.

chapters.title: Awake to experience
chapters.description: Mindfulness is established as being awake to this moment, including sorrow, happiness, and whatever heaviness is present in the heart.

chapters.title: Meeting pain gently
chapters.description: He encourages an undefended relationship to suffering and invites imagery or silently repeated phrases that evoke care.

chapters.title: Love meets suffering
chapters.description: The talk names the meeting of love and suffering as a home for the Brahma-vihara and points to the universality of pain as a basis for wishing others suffer less.

chapters.title: Turning toward love
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the abrasiveness of cruelty and offers a grounded encouragement not to dismiss the reality of love because it is incomplete.

chapters.title: Body as antenna
chapters.description: In the quieter close, the body is described as a kind of antenna that can tune the whole being toward compassion.

chapters.title: Planting non-violence
chapters.description: The practice ends with resting, planting seeds of non-violence, and letting love stay close to the surface in ordinary moments.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: The final note returns to suffering as a cause for deeper understanding.

title: Guided Meditation: don't wait to let go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with posture, settling, and a simple bodily yielding to gravity and breath. Matthew then turns toward impermanence, sufficiency, and the instruction not to delay letting go, before opening into a brief reflection on loving-kindness and the widening that can come when there is more space around grief. The final minutes include a personal remembrance of Kathleen Haviland, which grounds the talk in loss, relationship, and the practical compassion of practice.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling the body, meeting finitude, and not waiting to let go.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: He begins by asking listeners to find posture, suppleness, and strength, moving into a quiet settling of attention.

chapters.title: Yielding to gravity
chapters.description: The practice emphasizes giving body and mind to gravity and the earth while exploring breathing that supports ease and aliveness.

chapters.title: Finitude and enough
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on impermanence, suggesting that seeing finitude clearly can make each breath feel sufficient.

chapters.title: Do not wait
chapters.description: The central refrain frames letting go as something to do now, with surrender changing how everything is seen.

chapters.title: Resting after surrender
chapters.description: A long stretch of quiet practice follows, holding the field opened by the instruction to release and return to breathing.

chapters.title: Practice loving
chapters.description: He notes that love is needed because the human condition can feel too much, and invites a simple abiding in loving attention.

chapters.title: More space around pain
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward the body-mind as a place where experience can be felt more directly, with less control and more room.

chapters.title: Remembering Kathleen
chapters.description: Matthew briefly remembers a friend who died that weekend, using the memory to speak about grief, claustrophobia, and the relief of more space.

title: Guided Meditation: Abiding in Care
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on care as a quiet, stabilizing quality of mind and body. The practice moves from settling posture and softening tension into a broader sense of kindness, non-contention, and heartfulness that does not need to perform or fix anything. He then connects love with breath, grief, and the full range of feeling, suggesting that compassion can make pain more bearable without reducing its poignancy.
short_summary: A guided meditation on softening into care, letting kindness steady the body, breath, and heart.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with an invitation to notice what the word meditation brings up and to find a stable posture.

chapters.title: Softening Tension
chapters.description: He guides repeated softening of the body, agitation, clinging, and rigidity, using kindness as the means.

chapters.title: The Heart's Realm
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the heart as a sacred realm where kindness softens self-view without needing to argue with it.

chapters.title: No Need to Perform
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes that love and kindness do not need to be used as a strategy against fear, but can become a quiet ambience of non-contention.

chapters.title: Finding a Support
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to choose an image, phrase, or bodily sense that resonates with care and lets the body ease into that tone.

chapters.title: Love and Spaciousness
chapters.description: The practice expands into the body and awareness, describing love as something that can make awareness feel less held and more open.

chapters.title: Four Forms of Love
chapters.description: He names love in relation to goodness, suffering, good fortune, and helplessness, then brings the practice back to the breath.

chapters.title: Breath and Presence
chapters.description: The guided meditation continues with breath, silence, and repeated reminders to stay with the felt sense of care.

chapters.title: Grief and Poignancy
chapters.description: The talk closes by relating grief to love, saying that care can soothe agitation while deepening the poignancy of being alive.

title: Guided Meditation: Renunciation and Dana
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that moves from settling the body into a practical reflection on renunciation: laying down greed, distress, and the anxious project of managing life. He then links letting go with generosity, saying that when the grip softens, life is experienced as gift and the breath, body, and remaining life are all forms of dāna. The practice stays grounded and intimate, emphasizing relaxation, warmth, and the release of ownership and defensiveness rather than suppression.
short_summary: A guided meditation on renunciation that turns letting go into generosity, gratitude, and the sense that life itself is gift.

chapters.title: Opening and theme
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a brief welcome and an invitation to settle around the themes of renunciation and generosity.

chapters.title: What to put down
chapters.description: Matthew frames renunciation as laying down what does not bring genuine happiness while keeping hold of what does.

chapters.title: Unclenching the fist
chapters.description: He describes letting go as a softening rather than a forceful rejection, more like unclenching than throwing away.

chapters.title: Greed and distress
chapters.description: The practice narrows to putting down the anxious project of life and the greed, distress, aversion, and grasping that keep it in motion.

chapters.title: Letting control soften
chapters.description: As the mind relaxes, he points to releasing the need to direct each moment toward a preferred outcome and using the moment as an invitation to let go.

chapters.title: Ownership and gratitude
chapters.description: He reflects on ownership as a knot of greed and hatred, then connects relinquishment with softening defensiveness, gratitude, and generosity.

chapters.title: Breath as dāna
chapters.description: The meditation continues to deepen into a quieter recognition that warmth and goodwill make generosity feel natural in the body and mind.

chapters.title: Life becomes gift
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Matthew explicitly recasts breath, body, and life as dāna, inviting a poignant gratitude for what is received and given.

title: Guided Meditation: Everything is Passing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation centered on impermanence, beginning with settling, breath, and a gentle invocation of the whole history of practice. He gradually widens the frame to include reverence, loving-kindness, and forgiveness of conditioning, then turns to a clear reflection on anicca: not just that things pass, but that everything is always passing, including the sense of the observer. The practice closes by softening into the felt experience of breathing and the changing nature of awareness itself.
short_summary: A guided meditation on impermanence that moves from settling into breath to a direct contemplation of anicca, including the changing sense of self.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: The meditation opens with welcome, a call toward stillness, and the first instructions for arriving in the body and breath.

chapters.title: Calling in practice
chapters.description: He invites a deep breath as a way of summoning all the moments of Dharma practice and the qualities they have cultivated.

chapters.title: Reverence and devotion
chapters.description: The practice shifts into reverence for the path, goodness, and love, with a sense of bowing toward what matters most.

chapters.title: Loving-kindness and care
chapters.description: He broadens into metta and a shared field of care, grounding the practice in kindness for all beings.

chapters.title: Understanding wandering
chapters.description: Attention’s movement away from the present is reframed as a kind of innocent, naive search for happiness rather than something to condemn.

chapters.title: Forgiving conditioning
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward forgiveness, softening judgment around conditioning and returning to breath.

chapters.title: The flow of anicca
chapters.description: Matthew develops the central teaching that everything is always passing, including the apparent stability of experience and the meditator’s sense of self.

chapters.title: Melting into breathing
chapters.description: The talk closes by letting the idea of a solid observer dissolve into the changing flow of breathing and direct experience.

title: Guided Meditation: Emphasizing Tranquility
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on tranquility as something that settles when we stop adding effort and willfulness. He distinguishes between times when the mind is tired and needs nurturing brightness, and times when it is alert enough to rest in quiet, subtle sensations, silence, and ease. The talk gradually turns toward release, forgiveness, and a simpler sense of sufficiency, ending with a few bells and a period of settling.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting calm arise through non-striving, quiet attention, and gentle release.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a settling image from Thich Nhat Hanh and an invitation to let calm arise naturally.

chapters.title: Ceasing to Strive
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that relaxation comes less from doing than from stopping the urge to control the moment.

chapters.title: Yielding to Silence
chapters.description: He returns to the sense of silence drawing the mind inward, with a playful detour into a quoted line that is hard to place.

chapters.title: Finding the Quiet Beneath
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward yielding and sensing the silence beneath or beyond the noise of experience.

chapters.title: Brightness Or Rest
chapters.description: He adjusts the practice depending on energy level, suggesting either nurturing alertness or resting in subtle quiet.

chapters.title: Listening to Tranquility
chapters.description: After a brief pause in the guide, the meditation continues in the same spirit of open attention and receptivity.

chapters.title: Signs of Calm
chapters.description: Matthew notes that tranquility does not depend on total absence of agitation, only on noticing its signs.

chapters.title: Putting Down Burdens
chapters.description: The practice deepens into relinquishment, forgiveness, and the willingness to set down what can be released.

chapters.title: Enoughness and Closure
chapters.description: He closes by pointing to the mind's lessening hunger, Suzuki Roshi's reminder that being alive is enough, and the bell sounds that end the meditation.

title: Guided Meditation Presence is not a Transaction
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation centered on settling the body, softening effort, and letting the breath signal that the moment is safe enough to relax. The practice turns toward receiving experience without trying to extract anything from it, contrasting transactional habits of greed and aversion with a more receptive trust. He closes by broadening the frame: meditation is not something placed beside life, but a way of letting life rest within the Dharma.
short_summary: A guided meditation on softening control and receiving the moment non-transactionally.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: He begins by inviting posture adjustments and a truce with the body’s inevitable imperfection.

chapters.title: Breath as Permission
chapters.description: The breath is used briefly as a signal to the deep mind that this moment is good enough to let go.

chapters.title: Stories Held Loosely
chapters.description: He points to the frameworks and stories that prop up a sense of self, noticing both their stress and the relief of putting them down.

chapters.title: Worry and Safety
chapters.description: The talk examines the fear that nothing can be released, and invites hearing, breathing, and resting without tracking threat.

chapters.title: Enduring the Practice
chapters.description: A long reflective pause follows, with brief reminders that even when practice feels hard, the path is still a support.

chapters.title: Receiving Without Taking
chapters.description: He names the core teaching move: receive the moment non-transactionally, trusting it as medicine or teacher rather than trying to extract something from it.

chapters.title: Life Within Dharma
chapters.description: The closing teaching reframes practice so that life itself rests within Dharma, with surrender deepening into wisdom and love.

title: Guided Meditation: Clinging is complicated, letting go simplifies
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quietly structured guided meditation on how clinging complicates experience and letting go can simplify it. He repeatedly invites the listener to rest in the body, notice hope, fear, and thinking without finishing their stories, and soften into the breath. The practice gradually shifts toward gentleness and warmth, with an emphasis on meeting the mind’s movements without neglecting what matters.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing clinging, feeling the body, and letting go into simple present-moment awareness.

chapters.title: Opening and posture
chapters.description: A brief welcome and invitation to settle the body and gather attention for practice.

chapters.title: Taking up practice slowly
chapters.description: He contrasts a gradual inner settling with the possibility of putting everything down at once.

chapters.title: Laying down complexity
chapters.description: The talk frames radical simplification as something that can support deeper love and service.

chapters.title: Hope, fear, and the body
chapters.description: Rather than following thought stories, the practice turns toward the bodily impact of hope, fear, and memory.

chapters.title: Breath as release
chapters.description: Breathing is presented as a quiet invitation to let go into the present moment.

chapters.title: Clinging and stickiness
chapters.description: He describes how auditory thought can feel sticky when there is little equanimity with feeling.

chapters.title: Gentle warmth
chapters.description: The closing section emphasizes the innocence of the mind and meeting it with warmth, simplicity, and care.

title: Guided meditation in Style of Shinzen Young: Mental Images, Auditory Thinking and Emotion in the Body
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation works through Matthew Brensilver’s Shinzen-style framing of experience as seeing, hearing, and feeling. It begins with settling the body, then turns toward internal visual images, auditory thinking, and emotional sensations, showing how each can be known without becoming identified with it. In the final stretch, the practice broadens to the whole triad and ends with a gentle investigation of how suffering assembles beneath awareness.
short_summary: A guided meditation that methodically explores visual thought, auditory thinking, and bodily feeling as shifting sensory experience.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The practice begins by quieting together, easing the body, and finding a workable posture with some acceptance of discomfort.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and posture
chapters.description: The teacher names mindfulness as concentration, clarity, and equanimity, then invites a relaxed, upright embodiment.

chapters.title: Seeing mental images
chapters.description: Attention turns to the internal visual field, including fleeting images, visual thinking, and the sense of self carried in imagery.

chapters.title: Hearing inner thought
chapters.description: The practice shifts to auditory thinking, listening to verbal thought as sound rather than following its content.

chapters.title: Thought and orientation
chapters.description: The talk reflects on how language helps us track threats, opportunities, and time, and asks us to hear verbal thought without obeying it.

chapters.title: Feeling in the body
chapters.description: Matthew turns to emotion and affect as bodily sensation, noticing arousal, valence, craving, and clinging as changing physical patterns.

chapters.title: The three sense gates
chapters.description: Seeing, hearing, and feeling are gathered into one field of practice, with mindfulness meeting whatever appears and resting in blankness, silence, or peace when an object falls away.

chapters.title: Tracking the triad
chapters.description: A simple mental note of see, hear, feel is offered as support for moment-to-moment awareness across the three gates.

chapters.title: How suffering forms
chapters.description: The closing reflection investigates how suffering clusters around unnoticed sensory experience and how awareness can keep folding those patterns open.

title: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a lightly guided meditation that begins with warmth and goodwill, then narrows attention to a simple resting place in the body, breath, and sound. He returns again and again to the ordinariness of goodness, trust, and ease, suggesting that practice can make us less intimidated by the future and less burdened by the past. Near the end, he turns toward the relation between care and rest, pointing to the need for cycles of devotion and recovery rather than constant strain.
short_summary: A gentle guided meditation on goodwill, focused attention, and the relief that comes from trusting goodness and resting.

chapters.title: Opening goodwill
chapters.description: He begins by sensing the field of shared goodwill and the wish for less suffering.

chapters.title: Settling posture
chapters.description: The guidance invites a simple posture and a felt sense of sweetness and sangha in the body.

chapters.title: Goodness as practice
chapters.description: He frames Dharma as a gradual sensitization to goodness that brightens over time.

chapters.title: Trust and security
chapters.description: The talk turns toward trusting goodness, feeling less intimidated by the future, and resting more naturally.

chapters.title: Clear attention
chapters.description: He describes setting a gentle boundary around the meditation object and not making distractions into an enemy.

chapters.title: Resting now
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into resting with breathing, body, and sound, allowing the present moment to be enough.
