chapters.title: Offering goodwill
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to sense what kind of love is needed, notice hurt and sincerity, and offer phrases like 'May I be happy' and 'May I be softened.'

chapters.title: Softening into the heart
chapters.description: The closing instructions deepen the practice of loving-kindness and acceptance, including space for grief, fear, and the boundlessness of the heart.

title: Guided Meditation: The Breath and Awareness don't depend on you
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation that begins with settling the body and reframing the breath as something that does not depend on the usual sense of “me.” From there he turns toward awareness itself, emphasizing that it also is not owned or controlled, and uses the language of emptiness to soften selfing. The back half of the talk lingers in long repetitions and then opens into an image of egoic “proprioception,” showing how a sense of self is continually maintained until awareness becomes less identified and more spacious.
short_summary: A guided meditation on breath, awareness, and emptiness that steadily loosens the felt sense of self.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: Opening remarks, a brief schedule note, and an invitation to arrive and practice together.

chapters.title: Breath Beyond Self
chapters.description: The meditation begins by emphasizing that breathing does not depend on the constructed sense of me or on effortful control.

chapters.title: Awareness Receives
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from the breath to awareness, pointing out that attention and knowing also do not depend on personal will.

chapters.title: Emptiness Repeated
chapters.description: A long contemplative stretch returns again and again to emptiness, letting the phrase do the work of loosening fixation.

chapters.title: Ego's Bureaucracy
chapters.description: Matthew cites Trungpa Rinpoche to describe insight as stepping out of the bureaucracy and micromanagement of ego.

chapters.title: Same Stuff, Rising
chapters.description: He expands on self-related phenomena as part of the same flux as everything else, marked by the openness of awareness and the effortlessness of breathing.

chapters.title: Egoic Proprioception
chapters.description: Using the body’s balance sense as a comparison, he describes how the ego keeps tabs on itself and continually re-creates a sense of place.

chapters.title: Less and Less Me
chapters.description: The closing movement suggests that, as sensitivity grows, the self stops feeling like a home base and other modes of awareness come online.

title: Guided Meditation: Awareness and Effortlessness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores the shift from narrating experience to resting in awareness itself, drawing on Thomas Metzinger to describe silence as non-conceptual, non-reactive knowing. The talk moves from theory into guided sitting, where effort, choicelessness, and relaxation are treated as supports that can eventually fall away. Later he turns toward equanimity, frictionlessness, and the sense that the moment is no longer a problem to solve but something to trust.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting go of narration and discovering effortless awareness.

chapters.title: Narrative Self
chapters.description: He begins by noticing how meditation is easily folded into the story of who we are and what is happening.

chapters.title: Silent Knowing
chapters.description: Using Thomas Metzinger as a reference, he describes awareness as silent, non-conceptual, and free of grasping.

chapters.title: Instructions And Fruit
chapters.description: The meditation instructions are framed as gestures toward qualities that become natural when effort drops away.

chapters.title: Settling To Sit
chapters.description: He invites the group to sit, breathe, and relax whatever can be relaxed.

chapters.title: Friction And Equanimity
chapters.description: The teaching turns to frictionlessness, imperfection, and equanimity as a way of not fighting experience.

chapters.title: Effort Toward Release
chapters.description: He emphasizes that some effort may be needed at first in order to relinquish willfulness and choice-making.

chapters.title: Trusting Silence
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a quieter trust, where the moment stops feeling like a task and silence feels more central than sound.

chapters.title: Resting Safely
chapters.description: In the closing portion, he says life and awareness do not depend on personal control, and ends by noting the intended audience for these sessions.

title: Guided Meditation: Language as Safety
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation framed around a gentle critique of our reliance on language for comfort and control. The practice moves from settling the body and allowing imperfection, into resting with feeling and breath without immediately turning experience into a story. Near the end, he names the felt contrast directly: words can seem like safety, but deeper safety comes from wisdom, love, and learning to stay with uncertainty.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the body, resting with feeling, and loosening our trust in language as a source of safety.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: He introduces the talk’s central idea: meditation is not mainly about stopping thought, but about softening our over-trust in language.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The practice begins with posture, relaxation, and permission for imperfection before the deeper meditation unfolds.

chapters.title: Releasing urgency
chapters.description: He explores how relaxation reduces the urge to fix unpleasantness and helps prepare the heart for inevitable imperfection.

chapters.title: Rest without words
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward simple resting and noticing how quickly the mind reaches for words to soothe feeling.

chapters.title: Breath and feeling
chapters.description: Attention is brought to breath as direct sensation, with images and inner commentary gently set aside.

chapters.title: Uncertainty and story
chapters.description: He asks how much uncertainty we can tolerate before the mind begins to build a story, and distinguishes thought from the experience it tries to manage.

chapters.title: Letting go into silence
chapters.description: The teaching lands on the idea that language may not be making us safer in this moment, so the practice is to release into feeling and silence.

chapters.title: Risk and deeper safety
chapters.description: In the closing movement, he names the paradox that change can feel risky while words feel safe, and points to wisdom and love as the deeper refuge.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention, Effort, Choice and Selfing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation framed around a subtle paradox: meditation asks for attention, effort, and choice, while also pointing toward choicelessness, effortlessness, and surrender. He keeps returning to the way practice can strengthen a sense of doer and meditator, then gently loosens that grip by treating selfing as something to study rather than defeat. The guidance moves from settling posture and narrowing attention to breathing, through reminders and rebalancing, and toward a quieter release of the need to manage the meditation at all.
short_summary: A guided meditation on how attention and effort can support practice without hardening into a sense of doer or self.

chapters.title: Opening orientation
chapters.description: The talk begins by welcoming listeners and setting up the basic movement from speaking into guided practice.

chapters.title: Attention and effort
chapters.description: He names meditation as directing attention, making effort, and using intention, while noting how these moves can evoke self.

chapters.title: Selfing and practice
chapters.description: The teaching reframes self and not-self as different capacities, not opposites to win or lose, and prepares for the meditation instructions.

chapters.title: Settling with breath
chapters.description: Instructions turn to finding a sustainable posture, choosing an anchor, and gently returning attention to breathing and away from discursive thought.

chapters.title: Stability and reminders
chapters.description: He acknowledges ordinary choice-making and gives room for small reminders and support while stability develops.

chapters.title: Doer and deed
chapters.description: The meditation explores the natural sense of a meditator and object, then points to a deeper surrender that leaves less room for the doer to congeal.

chapters.title: Surrender to grace
chapters.description: Brensilver says surrender is not something the ego can accomplish by force, and invites a yielding to the Dharma's grace.

chapters.title: Beyond hierarchy
chapters.description: The closing repetition pushes beyond simple comparisons of self and not-self, warning that trying to rank them can strengthen the very selfing the practice is seeing.

title: Guided Meditation: Aperture, Vividness, Stability, Effort
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation built around four practical dimensions of practice: intention, attention, vividness, stability, and effort. He repeatedly returns to the sense that meditation is not a workspace for solving problems, but a period of putting down grasping and letting the moment be enough. The sit then moves from a more concrete anchor to a broader, more open awareness, with guidance on how to adjust effort without strain.
short_summary: A guided meditation on intention, attention, vividness, stability, and sustainable effort, moving from a concrete anchor into broader awareness.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the sit and frames the talk around a more concrete set of instructions than usual.

chapters.title: Intention to release
chapters.description: He emphasizes putting down greed and distress, and letting go of the urge to use meditation for problem-solving.

chapters.title: Attention and aperture
chapters.description: The guidance turns to where attention rests, including the metaphor of narrowing or widening the aperture of awareness.

chapters.title: Vividness and stability
chapters.description: He describes cultivating clear, vivid contact with experience while keeping attention steady.

chapters.title: Effort in balance
chapters.description: Brensilver explores the difference between sustainable effort and effort driven by clinging or control.

chapters.title: Settling into the sit
chapters.description: The guided portion begins by reassuring listeners that this period need not solve anything, and by softening the drive to optimize.

chapters.title: Anchor and soundscape
chapters.description: He invites attention to the breath, body, or soundscape, and stresses a gentle, sustainable way of staying with the object.

chapters.title: Returning from mind wandering
chapters.description: The meditation widens to include the mind’s activity, with instruction on noticing when agency collapses into thought and then reestablishing intimacy with the anchor.

chapters.title: Open awareness
chapters.description: The practice shifts from a concrete anchor to a more spacious noticing where the whole field of experience can be included.

title: Guided Meditation: What's Already Here?
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens with a brief reflection on war, cooperation, and the idea that peace may be something human beings have to invent. He then turns the talk into a guided meditation centered on a recurring instruction: stop looking, and let awareness, breath, silence, and even healing "find" you. The practice keeps returning to the ordinary fact that what we seek is already present, and that freedom and suffering meet in the same place.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting in what is already here, where freedom is found by letting go of looking.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: He introduces the sit with a brief reflection on war, cooperation, and the possibility that peace must be intentionally invented.

chapters.title: Freedom in suffering
chapters.description: Ajahn Chah's teaching frames the practice: freedom is found in the same place as suffering, so the invitation is to turn toward what's already here.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins with a sustainable posture and a deliberate shift away from efforting.

chapters.title: Letting breath arrive
chapters.description: He repeatedly invites the listener not to search for the breath, but to let the breath find them and suffuse the body.

chapters.title: Being found
chapters.description: The emphasis deepens into a release of duality and self-doubt as the experience of being found softens the sense of a seeker.

chapters.title: Resting in presence
chapters.description: He returns to the theme that freedom and suffering share the same place, encouraging the body to embody that freedom rather than pursue it.

chapters.title: Healing the search
chapters.description: The practice closes by resting in what is already here, with stillness and silence offering a gentle healing of fundamental alienation.

title: Guided Meditation: Faith in Silence
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on learning to trust silence instead of using thought as a constant coping strategy. He gently argues that the body’s signals, the mind’s reactions, and the urge to solve everything do not need to be read as threats, and he repeatedly returns to the reassurance that it is okay to let things rest. The practice moves from settling the posture and soothing the body into longer stretches of silence, with reminders to soften, stop over-reading experience, and listen for what tranquility makes possible.
short_summary: A guided meditation on trusting silence, releasing coping, and letting experience settle without over-interpreting it.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He opens with the framing of the practice, naming coping and inviting a posture that can be sustained.

chapters.title: Soothing the Body
chapters.description: The practice begins with embodied easing, fuller breaths, and softening tension in the face and belly.

chapters.title: Putting Down Complications
chapters.description: He reassures listeners that it is not neglect to set aside problems for a while and rest in tranquility.

chapters.title: Faith in Silence
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward trusting silence, letting go, and noticing that the present moment is not trying to harm us.

chapters.title: Over-Reading Experience
chapters.description: He points to the habit of making too much meaning out of sensations and phenomena, then invites a brief stopping.

chapters.title: Heart Training
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes how innocent the mind’s scrambling can be, and how much the heart can be trained.

chapters.title: Listening To Silence
chapters.description: A long guided period follows, in which listeners are asked to listen to silence, take cues from it, and keep letting go.

chapters.title: Extended Quiet
chapters.description: The meditation continues into a sustained stretch of silence with only faint, unclear audio artifacts near the end.

title: Guided Meditation: Lightly Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a lightly guided meditation that keeps returning to receptive listening: allowing experience to be received before it hardens into resistance or identification. The talk moves from settling the body, to noticing the quiet agenda of “me” in meditation, to softening around wanting, control, and the effort to make something happen. Near the end, he frames practice in terms of faith — the possibility that something can hold us even when control is released.
short_summary: A gently guided meditation on receptive awareness, loosening control, and trusting what remains when effort softens.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with posture and a simple invitation to become receptive to Dharma and to one’s own wisdom.

chapters.title: Deep Listening
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the idea that what is not received in awareness becomes identification, urging a listening that does not turn into resistance.

chapters.title: The Project of Me
chapters.description: Awareness notices the subtle tension of trying to make meditation happen and the transactional mind that issues demands to the moment.

chapters.title: Wanting and Agenda
chapters.description: The practice turns toward background wanting, bargaining with the present, and the way knowing becomes entangled with those agendas.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: He points toward a confidence that remains after effort and strategy dissolve, encouraging trust in awareness that does not depend on the self.

chapters.title: Control or Connection
chapters.description: Using Michelle McDonald’s question, he explores whether practice is driven by control or connection and suggests that release is possible.

chapters.title: Faith in Letting Go
chapters.description: The meditation closes with repeated reassurance that something can hold us beyond control, ending with a quiet emphasis on release and willingness.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention as Prayer
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames this guided meditation around Simone Weil’s line that attention at its highest degree is prayer, then unfolds that idea through body, breath, and sound. The practice moves from a somewhat deliberate, instructed beginning toward a softer sense that awareness is not owned or controlled by a separate meditator. Along the way he weaves in forgiveness, devotion, and the gradual loosening of selfing until attention feels more like surrender than effort.
short_summary: A guided meditation exploring how attention, when intimate enough, can become prayer and dissolve the sense of a separate doer.

chapters.title: Attention as Prayer
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Simone Weil’s line and names it as the conceptual pivot for the meditation.

chapters.title: View and Devotion
chapters.description: He explains how wise view and attention converge, and how the practice takes on a devotional quality.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The guided practice begins with posture, relaxation, and a gentle anchoring in body, breath, or sound.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and Recommitment
chapters.description: He invites forgiveness for imperfection and then recommits attention to the sacred within ordinary practice.

chapters.title: Love in Disguise
chapters.description: Attention is described as a form of love that can appear as worry, protectiveness, or longing for safety.

chapters.title: Less Control, More Grace
chapters.description: The language turns toward the clunkiness of selfing and the felt shift from effortful directing to receptive awareness.

chapters.title: Letting Awareness Find Us
chapters.description: He returns repeatedly to the sense that the meditator can soften, making room for awareness that is not possessed.

chapters.title: Groundless and Held
chapters.description: The close names self as agenda and responsibility, then ends with trust that when we let go, something will catch us.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention and Sustaining Openness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on two linked skills: training attention and sustaining openness when intensity arises. The talk repeatedly returns to how awareness narrows into problem-solving and how practice can gently re-open the field, with attention to the body, breath, soundscape, and a sense of okayness. Rather than building a conceptual map, it keeps pointing back to direct experience, patience, and relinquishment as ways to meet ordinary and subtle stress.
short_summary: A guided meditation on how to steady attention without letting awareness collapse into problem-solving.

chapters.title: Practice frame
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the distinction between focusing attention and staying open, and explains why the practice looks at small moments of collapse.

chapters.title: Settle into posture
chapters.description: He invites the sit, encourages a simple posture, and points toward trusting enough goodness to begin.

chapters.title: Intensity and collapse
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward how life’s intensity narrows awareness into problem-solving, and how to notice that movement gently.

chapters.title: Reverence for dukkha
chapters.description: He names the First Noble Truth and explores how attention to breath, body, and sound can make the mind less brittle.

chapters.title: Good enough awareness
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes patience, forgiveness, and a quieter relation to the urge to optimize or fix the moment.

chapters.title: Re-establish openness
chapters.description: He suggests returning to okayness, letting energy unwind, and rebuilding the open field before leaning again on an anchor.

chapters.title: Not solving
chapters.description: The closing reflection points to the simple relief of not solving everything and resting in awareness instead.

title: Guided Meditation: Establishing Tranquility
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation settles into posture, then turns attention toward silence as a quieter guide than the mind's usual instructions. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly narrows the attentional field to the breath, inviting the listener to relax what can be relaxed, allow what remains, and feel how even a small measure of tranquility changes the way experience appears. Near the end, he connects that shift to a Buddhist teaching that mind conditions what is known, closing with a gentle emphasis on refuge, nourishment, and a softer inner gaze.
short_summary: A guided meditation on narrowing attention to the breath, softening effort, and discovering how tranquility changes perception.

chapters.title: Settling Into Silence
chapters.description: The meditation begins by settling into posture and listening for what silence can reveal instead of immediately telling the mind what to do.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Body
chapters.description: He invites a simple release of tension, while allowing whatever cannot relax yet to remain without struggle.

chapters.title: Tranquility Changes View
chapters.description: The talk opens out into the idea that even a little tranquility changes perspective, without making life's difficulties disappear.

chapters.title: Narrowing Attention
chapters.description: Attention is described as a spotlight: rather than rejecting experience, the practice makes room for a smaller field centered on the breath.

chapters.title: Faith in Silence
chapters.description: Matthew names the fatigue of constant problem-solving and invites a kind of non-worry, softening into a smaller aperture of attention.

chapters.title: Stabilizing the Inner Gaze
chapters.description: With eyes closed, the listener is guided to steady a gaze that often scans for threat and to feel nourished by breathing.

chapters.title: Tranquility as Refuge
chapters.description: He links hyperarousal and suffering to the need for refuge, then closes by citing the Buddha on how mind shapes experience.

chapters.title: Closing Bells
chapters.description: The meditation ends with bells, leaving time for the quieter state to be felt.

title: Guided Meditation - Deep Letting Go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation explores a deep kind of letting go by loosening the sense of being stuck in time, self-story, and reactive wanting. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly invites the listener to rest outside memory and possibility, and to notice how clinging helps create the very sense of time it seems to live within. The practice moves gradually from a question of heart posture into spaciousness, emptiness, and a quieter encounter with experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting go of time, self-models, and reactive clinging to rest in a more open awareness.

chapters.title: Opening question
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a direct invitation to consider the heart’s posture if this moment were to last forever.

chapters.title: Letting go inwardly
chapters.description: The practice turns toward deep release, gently questioning self-hatred, self-view, self-love, and self-care as fixed positions.

chapters.title: Outside memory and future
chapters.description: Matthew introduces 'futurelessness' to point to the burden created by time and asks the listener to sense life between memory and possibility.

chapters.title: Time and clinging
chapters.description: He explores time as impermanence and notes that clinging and time reinforce each other through a subtle habit of reference.

chapters.title: Dropping reference points
chapters.description: The meditation repeatedly invites the listener to stop reiterating reference points such as me and you, self and world, past and future.

chapters.title: Becoming empty
chapters.description: The practice emphasizes emptiness as a way of not letting thoughts, feelings, and sensations strike or harden into the heart.

chapters.title: Hope and fear soften
chapters.description: Wanting and not wanting are treated as ways the present freezes, and awareness is used to melt the stiffness of hope and fear.

chapters.title: Putting down self-models
chapters.description: The close asks the listener to release all models of self and discover what remains when identity is no longer being assembled.

title: Guided Meditation - Mindfulness of Feeling
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on mindfulness of feeling, beginning with rain, body awareness, and the simple instruction to let go. He then turns toward the Buddha’s teaching that all dharma converge on feeling, using it to distinguish raw feeling from the stories, urgencies, and self-judgments that can grow around it. The practice moves gently from soothing and breathing with sensation toward equanimity, listening, and a more loving relationship to whatever arises.
short_summary: A guided meditation on noticing feeling directly, loosening the urge to react, and meeting experience with equanimity and love.

chapters.title: Settling with Rain
chapters.description: The meditation opens by inviting the listener to rest with the sound of rain and begin softening into the body and the room.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: Repeated phrases of letting go create a long settling phase, with attention easing away from effort and toward simple presence.

chapters.title: Feeling as Dharma
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Buddha’s teaching that sight, sound, thought, and sensation all converge on feeling, making feeling the center of practice.

chapters.title: Body and Affect
chapters.description: He describes feeling as something grounded in the body, with pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral tones, often sensed through the front of the body or as a wider body-wide atmosphere.

chapters.title: The Inner Meter
chapters.description: After a silence, he explores how feeling can seem to tell us what is right or wrong in a moment and invites attention away from that judgment toward the feeling itself.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Soothing
chapters.description: Equanimity is presented as a changing relationship to experience, where we breathe into arousal or numbness and let the tides of feeling be felt without obeying them.

chapters.title: Anchors and Urgency
chapters.description: The practice widens to include breath, body, and soundscape as possible anchors while urgency, fidgeting, and anxious self-story are met with patient attention.

chapters.title: Listening Instead of Reacting
chapters.description: When feeling seems to demand action, the instruction is to respond gently and listen rather than immediately follow the impulse.

chapters.title: Feeling into Love
chapters.description: The close emphasizes that feeling met with wisdom and equanimity can open into love, peace, compassion, joy, and kindness.

title: Guided Meditation - Landing in the Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that moves from settling fear and bracing into a close, steady contact with breathing. He emphasizes that discomfort, thought, and uncertainty can be met as present experience rather than warnings about the future, and gradually opens into walking meditation and the continuity of mindfulness across posture changes. The talk closes by normalizing the ordinary difficulties of retreat and framing surrender, simplicity, and patience as part of the practice itself.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing into breathing, meeting fear and distraction, and extending mindfulness into walking practice and retreat life.

chapters.title: Settling the Heart
chapters.description: The meditation begins by noticing bracing or fear and inviting the body and heart to soften with kindness.

chapters.title: Thoughts as Predictions
chapters.description: He reframes discomfort and worry as predictions about the future, then returns attention to present-moment experience.

chapters.title: Body Aliveness
chapters.description: Attention widens to the changing sensations of the body, including the subtle blur of experience with eyes closed.

chapters.title: Finding the Breath
chapters.description: He gathers awareness around the sensations of breathing and encourages a precise, steady contact with inhale and exhale.

chapters.title: Returning Without Judgment
chapters.description: The practice deepens into letting the rest of experience arise in the background while gently returning from narrative to presence.

chapters.title: Retreat Mind States
chapters.description: He names common energies of sleepiness, restlessness, desire, aversion, and doubt, and describes retreat as a place where all moments can be held in awareness.

chapters.title: The Obstacle Is Path
chapters.description: Using a teaching attributed to the Buddha, he normalizes the full range of mind states and says what looks like interference is part of the path.

chapters.title: Walking and Momentum
chapters.description: He turns to walking meditation, explaining how to meet movement, boredom, and visual impressions with continuity, and how practice builds quietly over time.

chapters.title: Surrender and Simplicity
chapters.description: The talk ends with a plain invitation to surrender to the retreat’s logic and trust the long practice tradition behind it.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting In Care
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on resting in care, beginning by inviting a felt sense of love, gratitude, compassion, or even grief as a doorway into the heart. From there he explores how care can color the whole body and make each breath feel significant, then turns toward the freedom that comes when love is steady enough to hold what is not love without forcing anything. The practice closes with a gentle wish for release from suffering.
short_summary: A guided meditation on beginning in heartfulness, letting care suffuse the body, and resting in a stable love that can hold difficult states without strain.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The talk opens with a brief welcome and a transition into stillness.

chapters.title: Begin With Love
chapters.description: He invites a starting point of love, gratitude, compassion, joy, equanimity, or grief as access to the heart.

chapters.title: Care Flavors Experience
chapters.description: He points out how love, like irritation or sadness, can color everything, even the breath.

chapters.title: Body Suffused With Care
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a more embodied sense of tenderness and aliveness spread through the body.

chapters.title: Freedom Still Beckons
chapters.description: He reflects on the promise that practice is possible in any moment and that love and freedom are not foreclosed.

chapters.title: Steadier Love
chapters.description: The meditation emphasizes letting love become less ambivalent and more stable, without needing force.

chapters.title: Holding What Is Not Love
chapters.description: When difficult states arise, they can be placed into a larger container of love rather than fought or suppressed.

chapters.title: Closing Wish
chapters.description: The meditation ends with a gentle blessing for freedom from suffering.

title: Guided Meditation: Resting In What We Cannot Stop
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on resting in awareness as something that is already present and not fully under personal control. The talk moves from settling the body and appreciating being alive, into a careful distinction between the effort of practice and the effortlessness of awareness itself. He repeatedly points back to the phrase that whatever cannot be stopped is where to rest, treating mindfulness as a kind of grace rather than a project of will.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling effort, noticing awareness, and resting in what cannot be stopped.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with posture, settling, and a gentle invitation to arrive in the room.

chapters.title: Gratitude for Awareness
chapters.description: He turns toward gratitude for being alive and conscious, and toward the improbable conditions that allow awareness at all.

chapters.title: Effort and Ease
chapters.description: Brensilver distinguishes the practical effort of directing attention from the deeper ease of letting things be.

chapters.title: Awareness Beyond Will
chapters.description: He explores awareness as something that does not depend on will, cannot be forced, and does not cost attention in the usual way.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as Grace
chapters.description: The talk lingers on the sense that mindfulness blooms and collapses without being manufactured, revealing the limits of self-control.

chapters.title: Resting in What Cannot Stop
chapters.description: The meditation closes by inviting rest in the awareness that cannot be stopped and therefore does not need to be managed.

title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Moment Soften You
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on the felt sense of softening rather than forcing concentration. He moves through breath, body, thought, emotion, and sound as different ways the moment can be met, with repeated encouragement to meet experience with blessing, awareness, and release. The practice ends by framing this softening as rehearsal for freedom: not pretending discomfort is pleasant, but learning to let go again and again.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting experience soften the heart, using breath, body, and awareness as ways to rehearse letting go.

chapters.title: Arriving
chapters.description: A brief opening welcomes listeners and settles into the practice.

chapters.title: Entering the moment
chapters.description: He describes different ways of coming into presence, whether gradually or all at once.

chapters.title: Finding the breath
chapters.description: The meditation turns to the breath as a way into immediacy, sometimes through effort and sometimes by simple availability.

chapters.title: Meeting the body
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to sense what hurts, what needs care, and how thought, anxiety, and emotion can be met with blessing.

chapters.title: Softened by phenomena
chapters.description: He broadens the practice to include all phenomena, treating thinking, sensation, and breath as conditions that can soften the heart.

chapters.title: When suppleness is lost
chapters.description: The talk acknowledges how identification and self-protective reactivity harden the heart, and how softness can return after contraction.

chapters.title: Letting go in practice
chapters.description: He lingers on the movement from clinging to release, including the way discomfort and relief can coexist in softening.

chapters.title: Rehearsing freedom
chapters.description: The closing frames practice as repeated rehearsal of freedom, like an athlete practicing a shot, without needing to force a result.

title: Guided Meditation: Renouncing the Narrative Impulse
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites steady attention into bodily feeling and the tendency to turn sensation into a story. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes renouncing the narrative impulse—not by suppressing thought, but by meeting feeling as feeling and softening the reflex to search for meaning, threat, or command. The practice gradually opens toward defenselessness, spaciousness, and the possibility that nothing needs to happen right now.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting bodily feeling without turning it into a story, and softening the impulse toward narrative and control.

chapters.title: Opening and Welcome
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a brief welcome and settling into the shared sit.

chapters.title: Inviting Goodwill
chapters.description: He turns attention toward the guided meditation and the sense of collective goodwill and gratitude.

chapters.title: Landing in the Body
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes breathing, arriving in the body, and becoming available to the present moment.

chapters.title: Feeling Without Story
chapters.description: He points to the body's feeling-circuits and explains the practice of letting feeling remain feeling without adding narrative.

chapters.title: Narrative and Vigilance
chapters.description: The talk names the natural habit of interpreting feeling through threat, opportunity, longing, and aversion, and the vulnerability of renouncing that habit.

chapters.title: Experimenting with Defenselessness
chapters.description: The meditation asks for direct contact with feeling, softening compulsive energies and the felt need to make something happen.

chapters.title: Nothing Needs to Happen
chapters.description: He guides a sustained period of resting with experience, opening to the possibility that nothing is required in this moment.

chapters.title: Distilling Wisdom
chapters.description: The closing notes suggest that this way of meeting feeling helps separate wisdom from compulsivity and supports living well.

title: Guided Meditation: Not updating your autobiography
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation and reflection on not turning each moment into a continuation of the personal story. He repeatedly returns to the body, breath, and sensory precision, then widens into equanimity, non-clinging, and a kind of futurelessness that lets this moment be enough. The talk moves gently between instruction and reflection, emphasizing surrender without erasing memory or life history.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the autobiography of self and meeting experience with more precision, equanimity, and surrender.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: Matthew welcomes the group and begins the meditation with simple settling instructions.

chapters.title: Body and Breath
chapters.description: He orients attention to the felt sense of the body and breath, not trying to make pain pleasant but to include it more fully.

chapters.title: Awareness Spreads
chapters.description: The practice continues with repeated invitations to sit and let awareness fill out the body, soothing hot spots where possible.

chapters.title: Not the Next Chapter
chapters.description: He turns to the main reflection: practice is not a continuation of the autobiography, but a surrender of that story-making.

chapters.title: Letting Go of Story
chapters.description: He names the many ways we keep refreshing identity through likes, dislikes, plans, and memories, and asks for more precise attention to present sensation.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Non-clinging
chapters.description: He reflects on how experience is always mixed, and how equanimity means not needing to sort everything into success or failure.

chapters.title: Self as Hologram
chapters.description: He suggests the sense of self can freeze through clinging, like a hologram cast by spotlights, and that non-clinging loosens it.

chapters.title: Futurelessness
chapters.description: The closing reflection invites surrender into a sense of futurelessness, a different way of learning that does not leverage the present for later.

title: Guided Meditation - The Warmth of Love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on warmth as a lived quality rather than a metaphor, moving gently from settling the body into the wider field of love, compassion, joy, and equanimity. The guidance emphasizes allowing, forgiving, and making a truce with imperfection, then opens into phrases of well-wishing and a quiet acceptance of what cannot be controlled. A brief closing chant may be present near the end, though its exact framing is not fully clear.
short_summary: A gentle guided meditation on warmth, lovingkindness, and openhearted acceptance.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The talk opens with a brief welcome and an invitation to sit together and begin with questions and dialogue before the practice.

chapters.title: Warmth of Love
chapters.description: Matthew frames warmth as something practical and immediate, then describes how rest and love support each other and soften the heart.

chapters.title: Allowing and Letting Go
chapters.description: He points to settling, allowing, and making peace with the imperfection of human life, letting the mind become more supple.

chapters.title: What the Heart Needs
chapters.description: The guidance turns toward sensing what the heart needs now, including kindness, self-forgiveness, and accountability without harshness.

chapters.title: Metta and Compassion
chapters.description: He names lovingkindness, compassion, and joy as different ways of meeting experience with care and recognizing goodness.

chapters.title: Equanimity at Limits
chapters.description: The practice shifts to equanimity: remaining open-hearted when suffering cannot be governed or changed by wish alone.

chapters.title: Well-Wishing
chapters.description: The closing guidance offers simple phrases of blessing, peace, and freedom from suffering, ending with a chant-like phrase that may be a closing invocation.

title: Guided Meditation - Love and Grief
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins by settling the body and then opens into a reflection on Leonard Cohen’s “Treaty,” using the song’s longing for peace as a way into the meeting of love and grief. Matthew then explores grief as something that may belong within practice, even suggesting it could be understood as a kind of fifth Brahma-vihara, before turning to impermanence and how grief can mature into compassion, gratitude, and reverence. The session closes with metta phrases and a final emphasis on how intimate contact with our own suffering can deepen our response to others.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling the body into a quiet exploration of love, grief, impermanence, and compassion.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The meditation begins by finding the posture and establishing a calm, embodied starting point.

chapters.title: Leonard Cohen's Treaty
chapters.description: A brief quotation from Leonard Cohen frames the wish for reconciliation between love and pain.

chapters.title: Starting the Practice
chapters.description: Matthew returns to the posture and reorients the session toward the lived experience of the body.

chapters.title: Love and Grief
chapters.description: He reflects on the claim that love and grief are inseparable and suggests grief may deserve more recognition in practice.

chapters.title: Grieving Impermanence
chapters.description: Impermanence is named both as a release and, at times, as something we may need to grieve.

chapters.title: Opening Into Metta
chapters.description: The meditation gradually turns toward kindness and prepares for a loving-kindness sequence.

chapters.title: Wishing Well
chapters.description: He leads classic metta phrases, offering wishes for happiness, safety, and freedom from suffering.

chapters.title: Compassion for Others
chapters.description: The closing reflection shows how repeated contact with our own suffering can make others' pain feel more immediately knowable.

title: Friday Morning Instructions
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how perception is shaped by motivation, then turns toward letting go as a way of seeing more clearly. He moves from the innocence of clinging and the body’s affective patterns into a steadier, more compassionate relationship with experience, including the repeated refrain that we are not doing our life wrong. The talk closes on trust: awareness is not a story, and when we fall, love may be what catches us.
short_summary: A talk on how wanting shapes seeing, and how letting go opens a less defended, more trusting relationship to life.

chapters.title: Wanting Shapes Seeing
chapters.description: He begins by pointing out that what we see and what we want are not really separate, and that motivation infuses knowing.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: The teaching shifts to letting go as a practical necessity for clearer seeing, while refusing the idea that this means approving future harm.

chapters.title: Clinging and Self-Knowledge
chapters.description: He argues that clinging blocks self-understanding and that awareness must meet the body and mind without preference or self-reproach.

chapters.title: Affect and Innocence
chapters.description: The talk examines how clinging arises through arousal and valence, then softens into the repeated reassurance that we are not doing life wrong.

chapters.title: Suffering and Delusion
chapters.description: He describes how bodily suffering, resolution, and persistence relate to greed, hate, and the ways delusion rationalizes them.

chapters.title: Mind In Images
chapters.description: The teaching turns to how thinking works through words, pictures, and bodily orientation, along with the many assumptions that shape the next moment.

chapters.title: Awareness Is Not Story
chapters.description: He emphasizes that awareness is not an autobiography and that there are many Dharma contexts where our usual knowing cannot be carried forward.

chapters.title: Faith and Being Held
chapters.description: The talk ends in a long invocation of trust: that the Dharma will not harm us, and that love may catch us when we fall.

title: Wednesday morning instructions
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet set of instructions for sitting with experience as something moving and changing rather than fixed. He keeps returning to posture, nervous system, and the practice of meeting feeling with awareness, patience, and sensitivity, then opens that ground into equanimity, love, and a frank view of the human condition. The talk includes a few clearly garbled transcript passages; the teaching itself remains steady and coherent.
short_summary: A grounded Dharma talk on receptivity, equanimity, and how awareness and love meet the intensity of lived experience.

chapters.title: Changing experience
chapters.description: He begins by describing experience as fluid and unstable, more like a blooming and collapsing process than something fixed.

chapters.title: Returning to the body
chapters.description: The talk turns toward posture and embodiment, with instructions to bring mind into the body and let relaxation begin there.

chapters.title: Thought as aftermath
chapters.description: Brensilver describes thought as something like exhaust from feeling, and introduces the practice of bathing feeling in awareness and patience.

chapters.title: Soothing the system
chapters.description: He speaks about stimulation, the effects of samsara, and how receptivity can soften and soothe the nervous system.

chapters.title: Being changed
chapters.description: Rather than trying to change the world, he emphasizes letting experience change us and digesting intensity before it hardens into story.

chapters.title: Equanimity’s by-products
chapters.description: He names the possible fruits of open awareness meeting intensity: tranquility, peace, and sometimes love.

chapters.title: The necessity of love
chapters.description: The teaching widens into the many forms love can take, the compatibility of awareness and love, and a sober account of wisdom, violence, and the human condition.

chapters.title: Awareness and helplessness
chapters.description: He closes by saying awareness practice is never far from love, especially where helplessness is most felt.

title: Morning Instructions: Landing in the body breathing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens this first guided sit of a week-long retreat by inviting listeners to set down the background project of self-management and settle into the body. The practice unfolds as a steady return to softening—face, shoulders, belly, and breath—along with a gentler relationship to attention, fatigue, and restlessness. In the second half, he widens the frame to walking practice, continuity through the day, and the patience to keep practicing when nothing seems to be happening.
short_summary: A grounded retreat opening on softening, body awareness, breath, and sustainable mindfulness through sitting and walking practice.

chapters.title: Arriving at retreat
chapters.description: He introduces the first full day of retreat and asks listeners to set aside the mental habit of managing the whole experience.

chapters.title: Soften the face
chapters.description: The guidance turns to relaxing the face, jaw, shoulders, and torso so attention can rest more easily in the body.

chapters.title: Let breath emerge
chapters.description: He invites the breath to become more prominent within a wider field of sensation, without forcing attention to grab it.

chapters.title: Interest over force
chapters.description: The talk reframes practice as nurturing interest rather than holding experience at bay, and as receiving moments of settled breathing as a gift.

chapters.title: Restlessness and fatigue
chapters.description: Listeners are encouraged to breathe into wanting, aversion, numbness, and hyperintensity rather than treating them as problems to overpower.

chapters.title: Trust the day
chapters.description: Matthew shifts from the cushion to the structure of retreat, emphasizing continuity, not making conclusions early, and blessing the whole day with awareness.

chapters.title: Walking practice
chapters.description: He offers detailed instructions for mindful walking, including pace, narrow or wide attention, and alternating anchors like feet, whole body, and metta.

chapters.title: Boredom and trust
chapters.description: The closing notes stress equanimity with boredom, waiting for results, and staying open when it feels like nothing is working.

title: Reflections and Guided Meditation on Mudita
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on mudita, or appreciative joy, by first grounding it in tenderness, grief, and the ways the heart can stay open without pretending. He then turns to comparison and envy as forms of suffering that support a false self, and shows how practice can loosen that grip through compassion, softness, and rejoicing in another’s welfare. The talk closes with a guided meditation that begins with an easy person, then moves toward someone who stirs comparison, and ends in self-care and blessing.
short_summary: A reflection and guided meditation on mudita as a joyful, noncompetitive freedom from comparison and envy.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a short talk about practice and tells a Zen story that frames tears, tenderness, and Dharma as part of heart practice.

chapters.title: Tears and tenderness
chapters.description: He explores how grief and compassion can feel close to the heart, while joy may feel remote or even disloyal to suffering.

chapters.title: Joy without pretending
chapters.description: Matthew describes how practice can make the heart more fluid and argues that Dharma does not ask anyone to manufacture joy.

chapters.title: What mudita is
chapters.description: He defines mudita as delight in another’s welfare and names comparison and envy as the main obstacles, bringing in a brief quotation about envy and the burden of measurement.

chapters.title: Selfing through comparison
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the ways envy reveals self-view, and Matthew repeatedly offers the line that anatta is the only true form of self-esteem.

chapters.title: Sharing another’s good
chapters.description: He explains that joy is not about ignoring suffering, but about easing self-construction so we can genuinely share in another person’s happiness.

chapters.title: Guided practice begins
chapters.description: The meditation starts with images and phrases of joy, then invites a person for whom mudita comes easily and the felt sense of partaking in their happiness.

chapters.title: Working with envy
chapters.description: Matthew shifts toward someone who is harder to rejoice with, encouraging close attention to contraction, comparison, and the felt signposts of self.

chapters.title: Softening and closing
chapters.description: The practice ends in forgiveness, release of self-importance, rejoicing in the other, and a final turn toward one’s own happiness and well-being.

title: Guided meditation: all phenomena as false alarms
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that keeps returning to a simple instruction: stop the extra doing, and meet experience with care. He frames sounds, thoughts, body sensations, and emotional surges as “false alarms,” inviting listeners not to turn every feeling into a story about the self. The talk gradually widens into a quiet teaching on confidence, emptiness, and love: if phenomena are not owned, they can soften the heart rather than harm it.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting all phenomena as false alarms, with an emphasis on care, non-narration, and the way emptiness and love meet.

chapters.title: Settling the Doing
chapters.description: The practice begins by noticing what can simply be stopped, especially subtle performing, pretending, and willfulness.

chapters.title: Care for the Heart
chapters.description: He turns the emphasis away from improving life and toward taking exquisite care of the heart in this moment.

chapters.title: False Alarms
chapters.description: Sounds, thoughts, and feelings are treated as false alarms rather than prompts for narration or self-story.

chapters.title: Confidence and Softening
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into trust that all phenomena can soften the heart and that the heart need not be harmed.

chapters.title: Emotion and Urgency
chapters.description: He points to the body’s emotional circuits, the impulse to fix or judge, and the use of awareness and love to bless those circuits.

chapters.title: No Story Needed
chapters.description: The practice becomes a sustained reminder not to read every sensation as meaning, but to let the moment simply be.

chapters.title: Love After Selfing
chapters.description: He says all phenomena held with wisdom terminate in love, and that stepping out of self-grip opens a freer, less possessive love.

chapters.title: Questions and Closing Guidance
chapters.description: In response to questions, he connects emptiness with love, then closes with practical advice about sensitivity, samadhi, and staying with the body lovingly and clearly.

title: Metta Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Afternoon Sit with Instructions
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver presents metta as something more than a pleasant mood: a capacity that makes insight practice bearable, especially when the heart meets the full intensity of suffering. He moves through the four brahmaviharas as four distinct kinds of love, then explains how metta can cultivate warmth, steady the mind, or bring buried grief and fear to the surface for healing. The talk closes with a simple practice of repeating phrases, listening for their effect, and widening the field of care to others without denying war, oppression, or loss.
short_summary: A grounded teaching on metta as the love that steadies insight, softens suffering, and widens naturally into compassion for all beings.

chapters.title: Love and Insight
chapters.description: Metta is introduced as essential to opening to the human condition without overwhelm, not as an optional extra to insight practice.

chapters.title: Four Species of Love
chapters.description: The talk reframes the brahmaviharas as metta, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity, each meeting a different condition of life.

chapters.title: Why Love Matters
chapters.description: Matthew explains why wisdom is incomplete without love and how metta can function as cultivation, concentration, or purification.

chapters.title: Meeting What Hurts
chapters.description: Metta is described as a practice that can draw grief, rage, and confusion into view and hold them with kindness rather than avoidance.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The instruction turns practical, emphasizing a sustainable posture, relaxation, and a quiet refusal to perform practice for anyone.

chapters.title: Remembering Goodness
chapters.description: Listeners are guided to bring to mind an image of goodness, sense the heart center, and let warmth soften clinging and self-harshness.

chapters.title: Abiding in Kindness
chapters.description: The teaching deepens into a felt sense that kindness makes it safe to let go and reminds the mind that aversion and fear cannot truly protect it.

chapters.title: Extending the Wish
chapters.description: The phrases widen from oneself to others, turning personal longing into a more universal wish for happiness, safety, and peace.

chapters.title: A Sublime Abiding
chapters.description: The close acknowledges suffering in the world while affirming that this species of love is not diminished by it and can be a noble dwelling of mind.

title: Guided Meditation: Deconstructing Sensory Experience
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation built around a simple three-part discernment: seeing, hearing, and feeling. After introducing the method and naming the tendency to turn practice into self-improvement, he leads a gradual scanning of visual thought, inner speech, and affect in the body, with repeated emphasis on noticing arising and passing without collapsing into identification. The practice closes by cycling through the three sensory spheres and resting in the untangling of “I am-ness.”
short_summary: A guided meditation on deconstructing experience into seeing, hearing, and feeling, then resting in the release of identification.

chapters.title: Opening the method
chapters.description: Matthew frames the talk as a more technique-focused meditation and introduces the three-gates approach he learned from Shinzen Young.

chapters.title: Seeing, hearing, feeling
chapters.description: He distinguishes visual thinking, auditory thinking, and emotionally charged body sensation as three separate sensory events and explains why that discrimination matters.

chapters.title: Settling and surrendering
chapters.description: The guided practice begins with posture and relaxation, then shifts toward surrendering effort, perfectionism, and the subtle violence of self-improvement.

chapters.title: Noticing visual thought
chapters.description: Attention moves to images behind the closed eyes, the mental screen, and how visual thinking can soften into blankness and tranquility.

chapters.title: Listening to inner speech
chapters.description: He turns to auditory thinking, showing how the inner voice can be heard as sound rather than obeyed, and how identification with thought creates drama and then resolves.

chapters.title: Feeling in the body
chapters.description: The practice shifts to emotional circuits and affect, with a reminder that even subtle feeling tones are significant.

chapters.title: Opening to all three
chapters.description: He invites awareness of whichever of the three spheres is most prominent, noting that more than one may be present and that simple labels can suffice.

chapters.title: Cycling and untangling
chapters.description: The meditation cycles through seeing, hearing, and feeling again and again, allowing mindfulness and equanimity to loosen the tangle of selfing.

title: Guided Meditation: Awareness Without Willfulness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that keeps returning to the difference between effort and willfulness. After a brief, candid opening about being emotionally caught up in a basketball game, he settles into posture, body, and breath, then invites a steady awareness of the residues of the day without trying to fix them. The practice gradually shifts toward relaxing the impulse to do something to experience, resting instead in an awareness that feels less personal and less effortful.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing willfulness and resting in awareness without forcing the moment.

chapters.title: Opening candor
chapters.description: He begins with a light, honest story about being caught up in a Warriors game before turning toward the meditation.

chapters.title: Settle the body
chapters.description: The instruction moves into finding a sustainable posture and arriving in the body with steadiness.

chapters.title: Taking stock
chapters.description: He asks listeners to notice the residues of the day and awaken to what is already present.

chapters.title: Allowing residue
chapters.description: The invitation is to relax and be willing to feel whatever remains without pushing it away.

chapters.title: Breath and balance
chapters.description: Breath is used to smooth, unify, cool, and enliven the body-mind as a way of gathering the field.

chapters.title: Effort and grace
chapters.description: He explores the role of effort in practice and warns against letting willfulness obscure the grace of the moment.

chapters.title: Relaxing the doer
chapters.description: The emphasis deepens into sensing the abrasiveness of always doing something to experience and relaxing that habit.

chapters.title: Awareness without self
chapters.description: Attention is contrasted with a more open awareness that does not taste like 'me,' ending with a place to rest beyond dependence on effort.

chapters.title: Closing bells
chapters.description: The meditation closes quietly with the sound of bells and silence.

title: Guided Meditation: Listening to the Mind of Buddha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that gradually shifts from settling into the room toward a practice of listening: first to breath, body, sound, and the darkness behind closed eyes, then to a deeper receptivity he links to Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines. The talk emphasizes trusting awareness, letting silence beckon, and resting without over-managing experience. It closes by naming practice as a way to stop endlessly coping and simply be awake.
short_summary: A guided meditation on listening inwardly, trusting awareness, and resting beyond the compulsion to manage experience.

chapters.title: Arriving and Settling
chapters.description: A brief welcome leads into the invitation to sit and settle into stillness.

chapters.title: Silence Beckons
chapters.description: He names the silence and begins orienting attention toward the meditative field.

chapters.title: Simple Objects of Attention
chapters.description: The instruction turns to breath, body, sound, darkness behind the eyes, and possibly phrase or mantra as supports for attention.

chapters.title: Listening to Cohen
chapters.description: Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines become a doorway into the teaching about listening beyond the speaker and toward what does not need to be forced.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: He frames practice as listening to the mind of a Buddha, emphasizing receptivity and trust in awareness rather than effortful control.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and Rest
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into forgiving the mind, resting in silence, and meeting sorrow with a more spacious stance.

chapters.title: Patience and Urgency
chapters.description: He explores the balance between spiritual urgency and abiding patience as practice matures.

chapters.title: Beyond Coping
chapters.description: The close points to resting from endless coping, releasing the habit of sorting everything out, and finding wakefulness in simple being.

title: Guided Meditation: Not Locating Oneself
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation organized around not re-establishing the usual sense of self. He invites a gentle stance of not knowing, then balances attention to the meditation object with attention to how attention is being held. The talk gradually settles into a quiet instruction to let self-references dissolve into the openness of the present moment.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing self-reference and opening attention without locating oneself.

chapters.title: Opening and welcome
chapters.description: A brief welcome and settling-in to begin the meditation.

chapters.title: Settling without knowing
chapters.description: The instruction shifts toward a gentle openness that does not fit the moment into existing ideas.

chapters.title: Not locating a self
chapters.description: Matthew names the habit of taking residence in familiar self-reference and introduces the central instruction not to locate oneself.

chapters.title: Opening practice
chapters.description: The meditation emphasizes releasing familiar reference points like who, what, and where I am, and opening in a new way.

chapters.title: What and how
chapters.description: He brings in Gil Fronsdal’s distinction between what we attend to and how we attend, suggesting a balance between the two.

chapters.title: Sustained guidance
chapters.description: The talk enters a long stretch of quiet guided practice, with the instruction carried mainly through silence and simple reminders.

chapters.title: Care and openness
chapters.description: He names a reciprocal sense of care between practitioner and moment, then returns to letting self-references dissolve.

chapters.title: Closing silence
chapters.description: The meditation continues in silence after the main instruction, allowing the open stance to settle.

title: Guided Meditation: Entering the Dharma Field
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle guided meditation that begins by settling into posture and letting the breath remind us of the Dharma. The practice moves from opening to the intensity of life, to finding refuge in breathing, and finally to entering a wider "Dharma field" where even mistakes can become part of the path. Near the end, he draws on Ajahn Sucitto’s language to reframe the meditation as stepping into a realm that continually recalls Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
short_summary: A guided meditation on posture, breath, refuge, and entering a Dharma field where all experience can become practice.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: The meditation opens with posture, welcome, and a reminder that simply sitting can evoke the whole path.

chapters.title: Breath as reminder
chapters.description: Breathing is introduced as a way to remember the Dharma and return to what is already here.

chapters.title: Opening and refuge
chapters.description: The talk distinguishes opening to life’s intensity from taking refuge in simple attention to the breath.

chapters.title: Breath as shelter
chapters.description: Breath is presented as a small but complete world, a temporary shelter for the heart and a place to set down the riddle of life.

chapters.title: Folding thoughts back
chapters.description: When the mind wanders into stories of life, memory, and anticipation, those movements are folded back into breathing.

chapters.title: Hope and fear
chapters.description: The breath is described as something that can absorb anxiety, excitement, and the body blows of hope and fear.

chapters.title: Invoking the Buddha
chapters.description: A prolonged repetition of the Buddha’s name deepens the devotional and recollective quality of the practice.

chapters.title: Entering Dharma field
chapters.description: Matthew cites Ajahn Sucitto’s phrase about entering Dharma fields and expands it into a view where everything, including mistakes, can remind us of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.

title: Guided Meditation: Trajectory of your life and trajectory of freedom
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames the meditation around two trajectories: the anxious, habit-driven story of a life, and the quieter training that leads toward freedom. The talk moves from this reflection into guided practice, emphasizing relaxation, sensing the whole body, and meeting feeling states without treating them as evidence that something is wrong. The practice repeatedly returns to softness, awareness, and the possibility that you do not have to become anyone in order to be present.
short_summary: A guided meditation on how ordinary life tracks worry and checking, while freedom develops through relaxation, awareness, and letting feeling be felt.

chapters.title: Two Trajectories
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the contrast between the trajectory of life and the trajectory of freedom, and how they can differ.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: He describes freedom as a training in interchangeable experience, where every moment invites release rather than control.

chapters.title: Settling Into Practice
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins with posture, body awareness, and relaxation in mind and body.

chapters.title: Undefended Presence
chapters.description: He points to mindfulness as being undefended against the moment and trusting the logic of Dharma over compulsive accounting.

chapters.title: False Alarms
chapters.description: The practice turns toward distraction, checking, and the body’s alarm signals, treating them as feeling rather than a problem to solve.

chapters.title: Softening the Heart
chapters.description: The meditation closes by letting feeling soften the heart and resting in the sense that no one needs to be performed here.

title: Moved by goodness: Reflections and Guided Metta Meditation (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins by contrasting mindfulness as deconstruction with metta as a rebuilding of the heart out of love. He then sketches loving-kindness as a boundless, noncontrolling form of love that is deepened by grief, and he places it within the four divine abodes. The talk moves into a guided metta meditation, first toward self-acceptance and then toward a quieter, gentler blessing practice that treats words as a bell for the body to hear.
short_summary: A talk on metta as a grown-up, grief-informed love, followed by a guided self-compassion meditation.

chapters.title: Deconstruction and love
chapters.description: Brensilver opens by describing mindfulness as taking experience apart, then turns toward the complementary work of reconstructing the heart through love.

chapters.title: What love means here
chapters.description: He defines this afternoon’s theme as love, emphasizing nonviolence, the dead end of hatred, and a free love that does not measure self or other.

chapters.title: The four divine abodes
chapters.description: He names the brahma-viharas and distinguishes metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity as different faces of love in relation to goodness, suffering, happiness, and the vastness of samsara.

chapters.title: Moved by goodness
chapters.description: He suggests metta may begin when we are touched by our own goodness and by the goodness in others, which makes hatred less tenable.

chapters.title: Cultivation and purification
chapters.description: Using a story from Michelle McDonald, he describes metta as something that can emerge after long practice, even quietly, and as a practice that brings hidden pain to the surface for healing.

chapters.title: Settling into practice
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins with sitting in a way that grants complete permission to experience what is present, without self-accusation or inner combat.

chapters.title: Self-blessing phrases
chapters.description: He invites listeners to imagine how a deeply compassionate being would see them, then offers gentle phrases such as 'May I be happy,' 'May I be safe and protected,' and 'May I be at peace.'

chapters.title: Expansiveness of love
chapters.description: The closing returns to the felt contrast between contracted suffering and the relief of love, ending with a sense that what we call self and other can dissolve into the field of metta.

title: Guided Meditation: Not reading the tea leaves of the body
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on meeting body experience without turning it into a puzzle to solve. He repeatedly returns to the idea that sensations, emotions, and alarms can be received as data, then gently released, rather than interpreted as signs of what will happen next. Near the end, he brings in the Mula Sutta to frame each experience as a doorway to Dharma, with liberation rather than self-analysis as the orientation.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting with body experience without reading it as a story about the future.

chapters.title: Opening into welcome
chapters.description: The talk begins with a broad, reassuring opening that evokes many inner worlds and the Dharma field as always available.

chapters.title: Receiving the body
chapters.description: After a stretch of silence, the instruction turns toward simply receiving the body as it is, without trying to fix it.

chapters.title: Not solving problems
chapters.description: Matthew warns against treating life as a riddle to solve and invites problems to dissolve in awareness, embodiment, and love.

chapters.title: Past and future pressure
chapters.description: He describes how the mind writes an autobiography of each moment and gets squeezed between memory and prediction.

chapters.title: False alarms
chapters.description: In the safety of the practice setting, body and mind alarms are asked to be held lightly, just for now, as false alarms.

chapters.title: This moment only
chapters.description: The meditation narrows into repeated invitations to place all hope into the present and stop reading the tea leaves of bodily sensation.

chapters.title: Letting go repeatedly
chapters.description: A long series of release instructions emphasizes that sensation, feeling, and notion can all mean the same thing here: let go.

chapters.title: Liberation in the sutta
chapters.description: Matthew cites the Mula Sutta to say that the core of all phenomena is liberation and that every wall can become a door to Dharma.

chapters.title: Renouncing words
chapters.description: The close returns to renouncing the urge to build the world out of words and staying intimate with body and feeling, neither heaven nor hell.

title: Guided Meditation: Settling and Opening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that begins with a very simple orientation to posture, embodied awareness, and relaxation, then gradually widens into advice for retreat practice. He repeatedly returns to the ways fear, hope, fatigue, and agitation shape the mind, and encourages listeners not to over-interpret what arises on the first day. The talk ends by extending mindfulness into walking practice and daily continuity, with a gentle reminder that one difficult day is not a basis for conclusions.
short_summary: A guided retreat meditation on settling into the body, meeting fear and uncertainty gently, and carrying mindfulness into walking practice.

chapters.title: Simple settling
chapters.description: He introduces a compact instruction: body in posture, mind in the body, relaxation in the mind.

chapters.title: Fear and interpretation
chapters.description: The talk turns to the questions that arise in practice and the need to sense the fear beneath them rather than rush to answer.

chapters.title: Breath as refuge
chapters.description: A long guided breath sequence follows, using repeated breathing and counting to settle the body and soften mental questioning.

chapters.title: Questions dissolve
chapters.description: He names common retreat doubts—whether one is okay, doing it right, or progressing—and invites them to dissolve in the breath.

chapters.title: Cycles of breath
chapters.description: Matthew describes noticing the precise stages of the breath, then reframes the practice as taking refuge rather than fixing oneself.

chapters.title: Retreat opens the heart
chapters.description: He speaks about the intensity of being human and, invoking the Buddha's teaching, says retreat can let that intensity break the heart open.

chapters.title: Meeting what arises
chapters.description: The guidance shifts to how the mind predictably gets caught in preoccupation, and how to keep going without making sleepiness or agitation mean too much.

chapters.title: Walking with continuity
chapters.description: He gives detailed walking-practice instructions, emphasizing continuity, broadening or narrowing attention, and meeting boredom without chasing stimulation.

chapters.title: No conclusions today
chapters.description: The talk closes with practical retreat logistics and a quiet reminder not to draw conclusions about life or the future from the first day.

title: Guided Meditation: Stability and Change
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation settles into the body, the breath, and the felt support of the earth before opening into a reflection on stability and change. Matthew Brensilver gradually shifts from simple instructions to a quieter contemplation of impermanence, asking what it means to rest in awareness without trying to hold experience still.
